set-up/cold-traffic-offer.title: Creating Your Cold Traffic Offer
set-up/cold-traffic-offer.content:
# Creating Your Cold Traffic Offer

⚠️ **Warning:** **This is one of the most important things to get right before you do anything else.** Most people skip this step, go straight into cold email, and wonder why they're not getting results. The problem is almost never the copy, the deliverability, or the list — it's the offer. Get this right first.

💡 **Tip:** This content is also covered in depth in the Cold Email section: Do You Have a Cold Traffic Offer?

---

## The Core Distinction: Warm Offer vs. Cold Traffic Offer

Most people come into cold email with a warm offer — an offer that only converts when the prospect already trusts you, already understands the problem, and already believes you can solve it. Warm offers work in referral and inbound. They die in cold inboxes.

A cold traffic offer works on a stranger. The prospect can say yes without:

- Trusting you
- Understanding how your mechanism works
- Believing you're good at what you do
- Being emotionally ready to commit to a full engagement

If the offer requires any of those four things to convert, it's a warm offer. It needs to be reframed before it goes into cold email.

---

## The Four Tests of a Cold Traffic Offer

Run your offer through these before writing a single word of copy. If it fails any of them, reframe first.

### Test 1 — Specific Numeric Outcome

There is a real number attached to the result. Not "more leads" — "8-12 qualified meetings per month." Not "grow revenue" — "add $100K in net margin in 5 months." The number can be a range or anchored to a case study. But it has to exist.

### Test 2 — Finite Timeframe and Scope

The offer has a defined end. "90-day pipeline accelerator." "First 60 days." Open-ended offers — "ongoing partnership," "retainer engagement," "we'll be your growth team" — fail this test. The prospect needs to be able to see the bottom of the commitment.

### Test 3 — New Money or Opportunity, Not Optimization

The offer produces something that doesn't exist yet — new revenue, new pipeline, new margin the business isn't currently capturing. Optimization offers fail because they require the prospect to already believe there's a problem worth fixing. That's a warm-audience assumption.

### Test 4 — Low Trust Required to Say Yes

The downside of saying yes is near zero. The offer structure has to be low-stakes enough that a stranger can agree to the next step. Pilots, proof-of-concepts, short paid tests, and free deliverables qualify. A 12-month retainer on first contact does not.

---

## The Language Tell

**Cold offer verbs — generative, net-new:**  
Generate, build, get, land, book, source, add, bring in, produce, deliver, create, win, close

**Warm offer verbs — optimization and management:**  
Optimize, improve, manage, streamline, enhance, refine, scale, grow, support, partner, help with, handle, take over

💡 **Tip:** **Quick test:** Read your current positioning out loud. If the main verbs are from the second list, your offer needs a reframe.

---

## The Reframe: How to Turn a Warm Service Into a Cold Offer

**Line 1:** End business outcome with a number and a timeframe.  
**Line 2:** The mechanism — "by doing X, Y, and Z." Line 1 is the offer. Line 2 is the service.

| Service Type | Warm Framing | Cold Framing |
| --- | --- | --- |
| MSP / Managed IT | "We manage your IT infrastructure" | "We'll cut your IT downtime by 40% and reduce support costs by $8K/month in 90 days" |
| Fractional CFO | "We provide fractional CFO services" | "We'll find $50K–$150K in recoverable margin in your first 60 days — or you don't pay" |
| AI / Automation | "We automate your business processes" | "We'll eliminate 20+ hours of manual ops work per week within 30 days" |
| RevOps Consulting | "We optimize your revenue operations" | "We'll add 2–3 new qualified pipeline sources in 90 days without adding headcount" |
| Cold Email Agency | "We manage your cold email campaigns" | "We'll book 8–15 qualified meetings per month with your ideal clients in 90 days" |

---

## The Wedge Offer

The cold offer's job is to get the meeting — not to close the deal. Your full-service package gets sold on the call.

- A pilot or proof of concept
- A first 90-day engagement with a specific deliverable
- A paid audit or diagnostic
- A lead magnet or one-pager that leads to a call
- A short, finite engagement with a specific numeric outcome

💡 **Tip:** If you're trying to put your back-end offer into a cold email, the campaign will underperform no matter how good the copy is. Build your wedge first.

---

## The Diagnostic Checklist

### The Four Tests

- Is there a specific number in the outcome?
- Is there a finite timeframe and scope?
- Does the offer produce new money or new opportunity — not optimize what already exists?
- Can a stranger say yes to the next step without needing to trust the sender?

### The Language

- Are the main verbs generative (generate, get, land, add, book)?
- Is the offer written to the end business outcome — not to the service itself?

### The Structure

- Is there a clear wedge offer separate from the full back-end engagement?
- Is the wedge what's going in the email?

⚠️ **Warning:** **Rule of thumb:** If the offer fails two or more tests, stop and reframe before writing any copy.

set-up/map-out-icp.title: Map Out Your ICP
set-up/map-out-icp.content:
# Map Out Your ICP

💡 **Tip:** This content is also covered in depth in the Sales section: Qualifying Prospects

---

## What Is an Ideal Client Profile (ICP)?

An Ideal Client Profile (ICP) is a detailed description of the perfect customer for your business — a blueprint for identifying companies most likely to benefit from and value your services.

- **Type of Business:** What industry are they in?
- **Location:** Where are they based?
- **Size:** How big is their team?
- **Revenue:** What's their annual income?
- **Decision-Makers:** Who makes the purchasing decisions?
- **Pain Points:** What challenges can your service solve for them?
- **Spending Habits:** How much do they typically invest in services like yours?

---

## Why Is an ICP Important?

- **Saves Time and Money:** Focus on the right prospects instead of wasting resources on leads that don't fit.
- **Improves Service Quality:** Tailor your offerings to perfectly meet the needs of your ideal clients.
- **Increases Customer Satisfaction:** ICP-aligned clients are more likely to love your service and stay loyal.
- **Boosts Revenue:** Targeted outreach leads to more conversions, higher close rates, and bigger deals.

---

## What to Consider When Building Your ICP

### Demographics and Basics

- **Industry:** What niche or submarket do they belong to?
- **Location:** Which country or region are they in?
- **Size:** Team headcount or business scale.
- **Revenue:** Annual revenue range.

### Behavior and Needs

- **Decision-Makers:** Who are the key players (e.g., CEO, CMO, VP of Sales)?
- **Pain Points:** What challenges do they face that your service can solve?
- **Spending Habits:** Average deal size (ideally $3K or more) and lifetime value.

### Market Insights

- **Close Rate:** How many leads turn into paying clients?
- **Market Size:** Is there enough demand in this segment to scale?
- **Case Studies:** Do you already have 2+ success stories in this niche?

### Platforms and Channels

- **Where Are They Active?** LinkedIn, email, specific forums, or other platforms they frequent.

---

## Market Research on Your ICP

Market research is the **single biggest difference** that separates average campaigns from excellent campaigns. Once you truly understand your market's pain points and how you solve them, your emails write themselves.

---

## Be as Specific as You Can

If more than 20% of the prospects you get on a call with are 100% qualified, there's something wrong with your ICP. Make it more specific and only agree to work with people who you're **certain** you'll be able to get results for.

---

## An Example ICP

- Niche — B2B SaaS companies offering Enterprise Software
- Average Annual Deal size — $10,000+
- Close rate — 20%+ on warmed appointments
- Minimum 2 outcome-focused case studies in the specific target market
- Total Addressable Market — 10K+
- Revenue of $3M per year / 10–150 employees
- Companies in the US, UK, Canada
- C-level & senior executives

---

## Prepare ROI Breakdowns for Different Scenarios

### Worst Case

- Close Rate: 20% — Show-Up Rate: 70%
- 8 booked appts → 4 meetings → 1 client (worth $10K)
- Cost: 5 meetings × $400 = $2,000 — ROI = **4X**

### Average

- Close Rate: 30% — Show-Up Rate: 80%
- 5 booked appts → 4 meetings → 1 client (worth $10K)
- Cost: 4 meetings × $400 = $1,600 — ROI = **5.25X**

### Best Case

- Close Rate: 40% — Show-Up Rate: 90%
- 4 booked appts → 3 meetings → 1 client (worth $10K)
- Cost: 3 meetings × $400 = $1,200 — ROI = **7.3X**

⚠️ **Warning:** Anything below the worst-case level means you can't provide ROI — don't take them on. Consider a downsell: sell them a guide to set up your service in-house.

---

## Refine Your ICP Over Time

Regularly analyze your best clients to identify shared traits and characteristics. Use this data to tailor your **offer**, **value proposition**, and **positioning** for similar prospects.

set-up/service-model-pricing.title: Your Service Model & Pricing
set-up/service-model-pricing.content:
# Your Service Model & Pricing

## Choose Your Service Model

---

### 1. Pay Per Lead

**How It Works:** Clients pay a set fee for each lead generated — defined as a lead showing interest in the offer or agreeing to receive more information.

**Pros:** Less operational hassle; low commitment for clients, making it easier to close deals.  
**Cons:** Clients can struggle to convert leads, reducing their ROI; risk of misaligned expectations around lead quality.

---

### 2. Pay Per Call

**How It Works:** Clients pay for each sales call booked or attended.

**Pros:** Tied directly to high-intent actions; increased value allows you to charge more; best beginner model.  
**Cons:** Clients may dispute billable calls; low show-up rates reduce earnings; hard to scale after a certain point.

---

### 3. Revenue Share

**How It Works:** You take a percentage of the revenue generated from your efforts. Best when expected call quantity is low but CLTV is high.

**Pros:** High earning potential; easiest service model to sell.  
**Cons:** Income depends on your client's close rate; clients can withhold information about closes; delayed payment.

---

### 4. Retainers

**How It Works:** Clients pay a fixed monthly fee.

**Pros:** Predictable income; fosters long-term relationships; lets you focus on results without constant performance pressure.  
**Cons:** Requires strong client trust upfront; may feel less flexible than pay-per-result models.

---

### 5. Selling Systems

**How It Works:** Clients pay a one-time fee for a system you build, then you ensure ongoing cashflow by running it for them, providing coaching, or supplying leads and scripts.

**Pros:** High upfront revenue; highly scalable.  
**Cons:** No recurring income unless paired with add-ons; requires competence across all aspects of lead generation.

---

### 6. Hybrid Models

Combines elements of two or more models. Most common: base retainer + performance bonus, or Pay Per Call + Revenue Share.

**Pros:** Balances predictability with high-earning potential.  
**Cons:** More complex to structure; requires detailed contracts.

---

## Set-Up Fees

Set-up fees cover the upfront costs of onboarding clients, building systems, and launching campaigns.

1. **Covers Upfront Work:** Onboarding, tech setup, strategy development
2. **Shows Commitment:** Ensures clients are invested from the start
3. **Balances Risk:** Mitigates your financial exposure when onboarding new clients
4. **Cashflow**

---

## Service Model Based on Your Experience

### Beginner

Sell **pay-per-performance**. Very little risk for the client, tied directly to results. Speeds up the process of signing clients and collecting case studies.

### Intermediate

**Pay-per-performance or retainer model**, or hybrid models to fit your offer.

### Expert

**Any model** you wish to sell, or a combination.

---

## How to Price Your Service

You don't pull pricing out of thin air — you reverse-engineer it from your ICP. **Price based on the ROI the client will receive.**

### Calculating the Price

CLTV × (Close Rate × 10) ÷ ROI you want to generate

### ROI Calculator

[Click here to make a copy of the spreadsheet →](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1GezWhb4DTmYv8-S-CRzXQNH7ZrOIGsJVA9VsuxiGxxw/copy)

💡 **Tip:** *"John, would you not agree that a 100X ROI is a great return? Based on your CLTV and close rate, we would generate you X every month and only charge you X. That's an incredible deal."*

set-up/create-vsl.title: Create Your VSL
set-up/create-vsl.content:
# Create Your VSL

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Coming Soon — VSL Resources & AI Workflow.** Video modules and an AI-assisted scripting workflow are coming soon. Stay tuned.

---

A strong Video Sales Letter (VSL) boosts leads, appointments, and sales across all niches.

---

## Why a VSL?

### 1. Transform Sales Calls

A great VSL pre-sells prospects, turning sales calls into easy order-taking sessions. Prospects come prepared and ready to commit.

### 2. Cut Lead Costs

Answering questions in your VSL can reduce cost per lead by up to 70% by filtering unqualified leads.

### 3. Data-Driven Advantage

A VSL built on real sales data is proven to boost conversions. Align your message with actual prospect questions for maximum relevance.

---

## Action Plan

- **Record Sales Calls:** Analyse 40–50 calls to identify recurring questions and objections.
- **Revisit Your ICP:** Deeply understand what makes your ideal client buy.
- **Script Your VSL:** Communicate your message clearly, showcase case studies, and address common objections.
- **Produce Your Video:** Confidently present the script in a high-quality video.

---

## VSL Length

- **Cold Traffic:** Under **3 minutes**
- **Warm Traffic:** Maximum **10 minutes**

---

## Tech Stack

- **Recording:** Loom or Wistia
- **Script:** Google Doc, Gamma Presentation, or PowerPoint
- **Visual Enhancements:** Hire someone on Fiverr for professional visuals

offers-guarantees-case-studies/offers.title: Offers
offers-guarantees-case-studies/offers.content:
# Offers

Your offer is not your skillset or service, but rather a claim of the benefits and end-results you can deliver.

An offer is a clear proposal to provide a specific service under specific conditions. It outlines what you're bringing to the table, sets expectations for the client, and explains the terms and results they can expect. A strong offer should be both attractive and transparent, helping potential clients immediately see the value.

---

## How to Truly Make a Great Offer

People who struggle with the agency often do so because they don't understand their offer. They stick to a **"Surface Offer"** — a simple statement that doesn't provide enough detail or value to stand out in the market.

### Example of a Surface Offer

*"We get B2B SaaS companies on the phone with their ideal clients every month on a pay-per-performance basis."*

This communicates the service, but misses the critical elements that make it compelling. To succeed, you want to create a detailed offer that goes beyond the surface.

### Example of a Detailed Offer

*"Our offer is tailored for [Sub-niche of B2B SaaS] companies in the US, delivering a minimum of 10 qualified leads per month through a pay-per-call model. We guarantee that the calls we generate will meet your qualifying criteria, charging only upon the successful qualification and attendance of the prospect. Additionally, we ensure a 100X ROI based on our clients' average contract value."*

---

## How to Create a Detailed Offer

Think about what a prospect would want to know: How will your offer meet their unique needs? What specific benefits can they expect? How are you managing their risks?

### Formula for Offer Creation

1. **WHO** — Identify your niche.
2. **WHERE** — If location matters, specify it.
3. **DO WHAT** — State the result: solve a pain point, increase sales, decrease churn, increase conversion rate, etc.
4. **PAIN POINT** — Address challenges or limitations that other providers might overlook.
5. **GUARANTEE / Risk Reversal** — Add a guarantee or promise to take on the risk yourself.

---

## The Thinking Behind an Irresistible Offer

An irresistible offer should be so compelling that prospects can't say no. Take on as much risk as possible, while qualifying your prospects well to minimise any real risk on your side. Aim for a **10x ROI** for the client.

- Build competitive advantages and unique selling points into your offer.
- Stack benefits that improve your offer without lowering your prices.
- Aim to have the best offer in the market, not necessarily the cheapest.

### Examples of Good Offers

**Lead Generation for B2B SaaS:** We flood B2B SaaS companies' calendars with meetings with qualified prospects on a pay-per-appointment basis.

**Sales Closing on Commission:** I'll close your inbound leads and you only pay a % of the deals I close.

**High-Ticket Coaching Programs:** I drive my own leads and close high-ticket sales. You pay after the money is in your bank account.

**TikTok UGC for E-commerce:** I'll generate 60 TikTok videos that will 4x your following and generate you $X sales in the next 30 days.

---

## Ways to Minimise Risk for Prospects

1. Offer a full refund if you don't achieve the desired results within a specific timeframe.
2. Use a month-to-month agreement rather than a long-term contract.
3. Lower or eliminate setup fees.
4. Provide a performance guarantee, where payment is only made after you reach certain results.

---

## How to Craft Your Offer Formula

**We help** [WHO — your niche]  
[WHERE — if it's specific]  
[DO WHAT — get rid of pain point / increase sales / decrease churn / get on more calls with qualified prospects]  
[PAIN POINT — or something other providers offer that's detrimental] in [TIMEFRAME]  
[GUARANTEE — If we don't do it in (timeframe), we refund your money / work for free etc.]

**Combine all these elements into a single sentence.**

*Example: "We help B2B SaaS companies in California get quality sales opportunities on a performance basis without relying on ads."*

---

## Closing Arguments

When you attract and qualify your prospects, there's no real risk to you. Provide a huge ROI — 10x at least. Build competitive advantages on top of each other. That lets you improve your offer without upping your prices, so you're seen as much better than your competitors.

The goal is to have the best offer in the market — one nobody else has. It doesn't mean you need to be the cheapest. It means you need to have the best offer.

offers-guarantees-case-studies/guarantee.title: Guarantees
offers-guarantees-case-studies/guarantee.content:
# Guarantees

You want to make your offer so good that your prospects can't say no. To do that — you need to reduce the perceived risk for your prospects.

The necessity of a guarantee depends on how good the rest of your offer is. If you're just starting out with no case studies, or are operating in a competitive market, a guarantee can significantly improve your conversion rate.

A good guarantee can take you from 5 to 15 calls per month alone. It cuts through the noise of empty promises, showcasing your confidence in your own offer.

---

## Types of Guarantees

1. **Performance Basis** — You pay after you get results
2. **Implicit Guarantees** — You pay as you get results
3. **Money-Back Guarantee** — You get refunded if you don't get the results
4. **Free Trial Guarantee** — You can ask for a refund in the first 30 days
5. **Free Work Guarantee** — We work for free until we hit the promised results

💡 **Tip:** We use guarantees as leverage to book sales calls. On the call, you may need to manage expectations.

💡 **Tip:** If you offer a guarantee, it emphasizes the importance of only taking on clients you're confident you can deliver results for.

---

## Stipulations

When using guarantees, you must have stipulations in order for clients to be eligible. Without them, you'll never make money. There are generally two ways to do this:

---

### State Stipulations

The client must already have something in order to qualify for the guarantee.

**Examples:**

- *The client must have a minimum of 10,000 subscribers to be eligible.*
- *The client must have a total TAM of at least 30,000+*

---

### Action Stipulations

The client must complete specific action items in order to qualify for the guarantee.

**Examples:**

- *The client must allocate a minimum ad spend to be eligible.*
- *The client must create an industry-specific landing page with a VSL to be eligible.*

offers-guarantees-case-studies/case-stud.title: Case Studies
offers-guarantees-case-studies/case-stud.content:
# Case Studies

Social proof, such as testimonials and case studies, is the single most important aspect when selling anything. If you can showcase that you've fulfilled on your offer to previous clients, you'll effortlessly attract new business.

Present your evidence in a way that is **clear, quantifiable, and outcome-focused.**

---

## Why Social Proof Matters

1. **Builds Trust Quickly:** Social proof provides a shortcut to trust by showcasing that others have successfully used your product or service.
2. **Overcomes Skepticism:** Testimonials and case studies provide evidence from third-party sources that back up your claims.
3. **Creates a Sense of FOMO:** Seeing others benefit from a product or service spurs prospects into action.
4. **Validates Decision Making:** Social proof reassures customers they're making the right decision, reducing buyer's remorse.

---

## Types of Social Proof

1. Interview Case Studies
2. Video Case Studies
3. Written Testimonials
4. Reviews and Ratings
5. Screenshots of results, booked meetings, client communications, etc.
6. Endorsements

---

## How to Present Your Social Proof

Be specific. Clearly reveal who the client is to add credibility. Ask questions to allow your client to explain in detail what their situation was before working with you. Then, direct the conversation toward having the client provide direct, quantifiable results.

| Weak | Strong |
| --- | --- |
| We worked with X to increase website traffic | **We worked with X to double the number of organic leads** |
| We did email marketing for X and boosted sales | **Our email marketing helped X boost sales by 20% in 49 days** |
| We booked 16 calls for X | **We helped X increase revenue by 30% by generating 16 calls in X days** |

---

## You NEED to Always Be Looking to Acquire Social Proof

So many agencies deliver exceptional results and ROI for their clients, yet hesitate to gather social proof. This is the lifeblood of your business — collecting social proof is non-negotiable.

Simply ask:

*"Hey [name], I'm putting together case studies to attract new clients. Would you be open to a quick video interview about our work together?"*

If they decline, compile the results yourself, create a case study presentation using Gamma, record a walkthrough on Loom, and post it on YouTube with SEO optimisation. Embed this on your landing page and use it as a sales asset.

Producing case studies should be a primary goal. It may take 3–6 months to build a strong case study, but every one of them significantly improves your booking and conversion rates.

---

offers-guarantees-case-studies/collectin.title: Collecting Testimonials
offers-guarantees-case-studies/collectin.content:
# Collecting Testimonials

This comprehensive resource will provide you with insights, tips, tools, and a framework to gather persuasive testimonials and case studies effectively. *(btw you could sell this as a framework to your clients!)*

---

## The Importance of Testimonials and Case Studies

Testimonials and case studies are essential for building trust and credibility with potential clients. They showcase your success stories, demonstrate the value of your services, and provide social proof that you can deliver results. In today's competitive market, incorporating testimonials and case studies into your marketing strategy is crucial for driving client engagement and conversion.

---

## Tips for Gathering Testimonials and Case Studies

- **Approach clients with gratitude and clear expectations:** Reach out to your clients and express your appreciation for their business. Explain how their feedback can help your agency grow and provide a clear overview of what you'll be asking them.
- **Be specific with your questions:** Ask targeted questions to elicit detailed responses. Focus on the problems your clients faced, the solutions you provided, and the results they achieved.
- **Make it easy for clients to provide feedback:** Offer multiple channels for clients to share their testimonials and case studies — email, phone calls, or video recordings.
- **Consider using video testimonials:** Video testimonials create a more personal and engaging experience. They can showcase your clients' emotions and authentic reactions, making them highly persuasive.

---

## Tools to Help Gather Testimonials and Case Studies

1. **[Testimonial.to](http://testimonial.to)** — An easy-to-use platform that enables you to collect video testimonials from your clients. It offers customisable templates, automatic reminders, and seamless sharing options.
2. **Calendly** — Schedule interviews with your clients for case studies using this convenient scheduling tool.
3. **Google Forms** — Create custom forms to collect written testimonials or case study information from your clients.
4. **Zoom** — Record video interviews or testimonials with your clients using this popular video conferencing tool.

---

## A Comprehensive Testimonial and Case Study Framework

### Framework Breakdown

1. **Introduction:** Provide a brief overview of the client's background, their business, and the challenges they faced before working with your agency.
2. **Problem:** Describe the specific issues the client was experiencing and how these problems were impacting their business.
3. **Solution:** Detail the strategies and services your agency provided to address the client's challenges, highlighting any unique solutions or techniques employed.
4. **Results:** Highlight the measurable outcomes and improvements the client experienced after working with your agency, using data and metrics to support your claims.
5. **Testimonial or Quote:** Include a powerful quote from the client that summarises their satisfaction with your services and the impact on their business.
6. **Conclusion:** Recap the key takeaways from the testimonial or case study and emphasise the value your agency brought to the client.

---

### Exact Framework with Questions

#### 1. Introduction

Provide a brief overview of the client's background, their business, and the challenges they faced. This sets the stage for the story you're about to tell.

> **Example questions:**  
> Can you provide a brief background of your business and your role within the company?  
> What were the main challenges you faced before working with our agency?

#### 2. Problem

Describe the specific issues the client was experiencing and how these problems were impacting their business.

> **Example questions:**  
> How did these challenges affect your business growth and sales?  
> Were you facing any difficulties with the quality of leads or lead nurturing processes?

#### 3. Solution

Detail the strategies and services your agency provided to address the client's challenges. Explain your approach and highlight any unique solutions or techniques employed.

> **Example questions:**  
> What specific services and strategies did our agency provide for your business?  
> Were there any unique methods or tools that our agency used to address your challenges?

#### 4. Results

Highlight the measurable outcomes and improvements the client experienced after working with your agency. Use data and metrics to support your claims.

> **Example questions:**  
> What improvements have you noticed since working with our agency?  
> Can you share any specific metrics or data points that demonstrate the results achieved?

#### 5. Testimonial or Quote

Include a powerful quote from the client that summarises their satisfaction with your services and the impact on their business.

> **Example questions:**  
> In your own words, can you describe the overall experience of working with our agency?  
> How would you describe the impact our services had on your business growth and success?

#### 6. Conclusion

Recap the key takeaways and emphasise the value your agency brought to the client. This is an opportunity to reinforce the positive outcomes and leave a lasting impression.

> **Example questions:**  
> Based on your experience, what would you say sets our agency apart from others in the industry?  
> Would you recommend our services to other businesses? Why?

---

By following this framework and asking specific questions, you'll gather persuasive testimonials and case studies that showcase your agency's expertise and ability to deliver results. This will help build trust with potential clients and contribute to your agency's growth.

## Step 2: Setting Up Domains & Inboxes

### Buying Domains

Buy your domains directly from [Porkbun](https://porkbun.com). You want to buy **lookalike domains** of your primary business domain — variations that look and feel like your main brand but are separate sending domains.

💡 **Tip:** As mentioned in the Deliverability Updates, .info and .com domains perform identically in terms of deliverability and reply rates. However, .info domains are significantly cheaper — so they're a smart default unless you have a specific reason to go .com.

Can't come up with lookalike domains? Paste your primary domain into Claude or ChatGPT and ask it to generate lookalike domain ideas. For example, if your main domain is yourbusiness.com, it might suggest: tryyourbusiness.com, useyourbusiness.com, emailyourbusiness.com, yourbusinessteam.com.

### Buying Inboxes

**Discount code:** CSS10 (10% off)

cold-email/campaign-setup.title: Campaign Set Up
cold-email/campaign-setup.content:
# Campaign Set Up

## How Many Campaigns to Run

We recommend running **2–3 campaigns** at a time so you can split-test different angles against each other. Each campaign should have a distinct angle or targeting approach — not just minor copy tweaks.

---

## Campaign Settings

### Sending Schedule

- Send all emails as **plain text only** — no HTML, no formatting
- Send between **8:00am – 6:00pm local time** (use the timezone that applies to your lead list)
- Send on **weekdays only**

### Key Settings

- **Disable open tracking** — open tracking hurts deliverability
- Maintain a minimum **50/50 split** between new leads and follow-up emails
- **Auto-pause the campaign if bounce rate exceeds 5%**
- **Stop sending emails to a lead once they reply**

---

## Inbox & Sending Settings

### Daily Send Volume & Warmup

Follow your inbox provider's recommendations (e.g. ScaledMail) for how many cold emails to send per day per inbox and how many warmup emails to run.

⚠️ **Warning:** Always complete the **full 2-week warmup** before sending cold emails at full volume. Don't shorten or skip it.

### Sending Delay

Set a minimum **30-minute delay** between cold emails sent from the same inbox. This reduces the risk of triggering spam filters from sending too frequently.

### Max New Leads Per Day

Set your **Max New Leads Per Day** to at least your target daily send volume. Example: if your goal is 2,000 emails per day, set the max new leads per day to at least 2,000. The default in most sequencers is 100–200 — if you don't change this, your campaigns will cap out well below your actual target volume.

⚠️ **Warning:** This is a commonly missed setting that silently kills send volume.

cold-email/manage-deliverability.title: Manage Deliverability
cold-email/manage-deliverability.content:
# Manage Deliverability

## Infrastructure Setup

### Inbox Provider Split

Buy **50% Google Workspace** and **50% Outlook** inboxes.

### Warmup Settings

- Warm all new domains for a **full 14 days** before sending cold emails
- We recommend keeping your warmup reply rate between **55% and 70%**, with a minimum of 35%

---

## Eyeballing Deliverability

You don't need expensive tools to know if you're delivering. Watch these three numbers in your campaigns:

| Metric | Healthy | Warning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Out-of-office reply rate | 1.5% – 2%+ | Below 1% consistently |
| Human reply rate | 0.7%+ | Below 0.5% |
| Bounce rate | Below 2% – 3% | Above 3% |

### How to Read the Numbers

**If out-of-office rate is 2–3% but human reply rate is ~0.2%**  
→ You're landing in the inbox. This is an **offer problem**, not a deliverability problem. The messaging isn't resonating.

**If out-of-office rate is consistently below 1% and human reply rate is near 0%**  
→ You likely have a **deliverability problem** and are not landing in inboxes.

You can use [EmailGuard](https://emailguard.io) to run placement tests — but eyeballing your reply rates is usually enough.

---

## Diagnosing & Fixing Deliverability Issues

Most deliverability problems come down to one of two things: **the offer** or **copy fingerprinting**.

### Before You Rotate Inboxes

1. **Change the messaging** — test a completely different offer angle
2. **Change the structure of the copy** — not just the words, but the format and length
3. **Send new messaging** and monitor for 3–5 days

If results are still bad after changing messaging and structure → then rotate inboxes.

### Copy Structure & Fingerprinting

ESPs are getting very good at fingerprinting email copy — not just by the words, but by the **structure and length** of the email itself. If you're sending a 3-line email and spintaxing through different words, it still reads as "a 3-line email" to the ESP. You need to **change the length and structure** periodically, not just the words.

- Avoid spam trigger words
- No links or images in cold emails
- Rotate the structure itself — not just synonyms

### Subject Lines

We've seen the best deliverability results using **just the first name variable** as the subject line (or close to it). First names are effectively infinite — every email goes out with a different subject line, making it far harder to fingerprint.

### Sequence Length

Keep it simple — **2 to 3 step sequences only**: Email 1 (initial), Email 2 (first follow-up), Email 3 (second follow-up, optional). Nothing beyond that.

---

## Advanced Deliverability

Hook your inboxes up to [EmailGuard](https://emailguard.io) and run inbox placement tests on a regular basis for domain-level visibility into where your emails are landing across providers.

cold-email/list-building.title: List Building
cold-email/list-building.content:
# List Building

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Placeholder Video — List Building Tutorial: Using AI & AI Ark to Build Targeted Lead Lists**

## Recommended Platforms

We recommend **Discolike** ([discolike.com](https://discolike.com)) or **AI Ark** ([ai-ark.com](https://ai-ark.com)) for building your lead lists. Both platforms support **lookalike audiences** — take a domain from a company that fits your ICP and plug it into the platform to find similar companies.

---

## Using AI to Build Better Lists

Use an AI tool like **Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini** to help structure your searches before going into the platform. Tell the AI you're building a cold email lead list and give it your ICP details (job titles, company types, industry, headcount, revenue range). Then ask it to give you a **comma-separated list** of all values so you can copy them directly into the platform's filters.

---

## Why We Recommend AI Ark

### 1. Built-in Email Verification (Including Catch-Alls)

AI Ark has a built-in email verifier powered by BounceBan. When you export leads, it verifies the emails in real time — including catch-all addresses. Data is fresh and reliable every time.

### 2. Personal Lead Database (Massive Cost Savings)

AI Ark functions as a personal database. First export of a lead costs 1 credit. Re-exporting that same lead later costs only 0.1 credits. This adds up significantly when you're cycling through the same contacts every 2–4 months across multiple clients.

💡 **Tip:** **Note:** AI Ark won't always find an email for every lead. Once you download your CSV, drop it into Claude or ChatGPT and ask it to remove all rows where the email field is blank. Clean the list before uploading to your sequencer.

---

## If You're Using Discolike or Another Platform

You'll need to run your list through an external email verifier before uploading to your sequencer:

- **BounceBan** ([bounceban.com](https://bounceban.com))
- **Millionverifier** ([millionverifier.com](https://millionverifier.com))

---

## Lead Scoring

Before uploading your list to your sequencer, **score your leads** to make sure you're only reaching out to relevant companies. The tool we use for this is **Lead Formatter** ([leadformatter.com](https://leadformatter.com)).

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Placeholder Video — Scoring Leads in Lead Formatter**

### How It Works

Upload your CSV from AI Ark or Discolike (which includes columns like Keywords, Company products & services, and Short company description) into Lead Formatter. Use an **AI enrichment prompt** to score each lead:

*"I'm [your business]. This is what I do and who I'm targeting. Using the columns in this list, determine whether each company is a good fit for me to reach out to."*

You can use Claude to help you write this enrichment prompt — just give it context about your business, your ICP, and the columns available in your export. This step filters your list to only the leads worth contacting before a single email goes out.

cold-email/writing-emails.title: Writing Emails
cold-email/writing-emails.content:
# Writing Emails

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Coming Soon: AI Cold Email Writer.** We are building an AI cold email writer bot trained on copy that has booked thousands of meetings. This will be available to all members of the Closing Clients System.

📚 Cold Email Frameworks

28 proven frameworks extracted from 210 top-performing cold email scripts. Each framework includes a plain readable example followed by the full spintax template for production use.

## Quick Reference

| # | Framework | Best For | Position |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | AVAERO Core Template | Lead gen / sales dev agencies | Email 1 |
| 2 | Event / Educational Invite | Product demos, B2B with teachable value | Email 1 |
| 3 | Stat-Led Pain Opener | Quantifiable problems, SaaS, agencies | Email 1 |
| 4 | Personalized Trigger (Enrichment) | Any industry with enrichable signals | Email 1 |
| 5 | Named Brand Case Study | When client has recognizable brand logos | Email 1 |
| 6 | Anonymized "Similar Company" Case Study | When no name-droppable brands or NDA | Email 1 |
| 7 | Playbook / Deck Offer | Consulting, strategy, audience intelligence | Email 1 |
| 8 | Custom Strategy Deck (High-Value Freebie) | Agencies, influencer marketing, creative | Email 1 |
| 9 | Competitive Displacement / Buyout | Replacing an incumbent vendor/tool | Email 1 |
| 10 | Competitor Frustration + Case Study | When you have migration stats | Email 1 |
| 11 | Direct Question Opener | Technical or niche products | Email 1 |
| 12 | Done-For-You System + Result | Managed services, agencies, outsourced ops | Email 1 |
| 13 | Pay-Per-Performance Pitch | Lead gen, appointment setting, pay-per-X | Email 1 |
| 14 | AI/Tech Replacing Labor | AI agents, automation, staffing reduction | Email 1 |
| 15 | "Already Built This For You" | AI products, creative agencies, pre-built demos | Email 1 |
| 16 | Personalized Campaign Ideas (Bullets) | Influencer, creative, social, content | Email 1 |
| 17 | Data / Research Insight Lead | Agencies with data, analytics, audience intel | Email 1 |
| 18 | Content Repurposing Angle | Content agencies, video, social media mgmt | Email 1 |
| 19 | Trend / News / Policy Hook | Offers tied to regulatory or platform changes | Email 1 |
| 20 | Urgency / Seasonal Window | Seasonal cycles, limited capacity | Email 1 |
| 21 | Missing Feature / Gap Spotted | Web design, SaaS, compliance | Email 1 |
| 22 | Free Mockup / Visual Deliverable | Web design, branding, physical product | Email 1 |
| 23 | Free Trial / Pilot Offer | SaaS, outbound, AI tools, testable services | Email 1 |
| 24 | TAM / Market Size Hook | Outbound agencies, lead gen, data providers | Email 1 |
| 25 | LinkedIn / Social Engagement Trigger | Any offer when LinkedIn activity is available | Email 1 |
| 26 | "Right Person?" Thread Reply | Universal follow-up / routing | Follow-up |
| 27 | Warm Follow-Up / Float Back Up | Gentle nudge after Email 1 | Follow-up |
| 28 | Soft Breakup | Final touch, gives an easy out | Follow-up |

---

## Email 1 Frameworks

### Framework 1: AVAERO Core Template

**What it is:** Opens with a specific result the prospect would care about, names exact ICP titles and verticals, adds light social proof, then a CTA with risk reversal and a tangible freebie. **Best for:** Lead gen / sales development / outbound agencies.

**Example:**

```
John,

My team can help you book 10+ qualified meetings per month by connecting you with Founders and VPs of Sales at B2B SaaS companies.

We recently helped a similar lead gen agency that was also struggling to fill their own pipeline.

On a short call, I'll walk you through exactly how we do it — even if you just want to take the strategy and run with it yourself. We'll also put together a list of 200 targeted contacts just for hopping on.
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME},

{{random|My team can help you|We can help you|Our team helps companies like yours}} {{random|source|generate|add|book}} [SPECIFIC RESULT] {{random|per quarter|per month}} by {{random|connecting you with|getting you in front of|reaching}} [ICP TITLES] at [VERTICALS].

{{random|We recently helped|We just helped|We worked with}} a similar [business type] {{random|that also|who also}} [similarity to prospect].

On a {{random|short call|brief call|quick intro}}, I'll {{random|break down|walk you through|show you}} how we do it - even if you {{random|end up doing it in-house|decide to handle it yourself}}. {{random|We'll also put together|We'll also send over|Plus we'll send}} a list of [NUMBER] targeted contacts just for {{random|hopping on|the time|the call}}.
```

---

### Framework 2: Event / Educational Invite

**What it is:** Invites prospect to a short educational session with an incentive. Asks for attendance, not a sale. **Best for:** Product companies with demo-able solutions, technical offerings.

**Example:**

```
John – projects ramping up?

We're hosting a 30-min Lunch & Learn on our new panel system, covering updated specs, install steps, cost math, and recent case studies so you can streamline upcoming builds.

Lunch is on us.

Does next Wednesday or Thursday afternoon suit your team?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – {{random|projects ramping up?|got upcoming builds in motion?|gearing up for new projects?}}

We're hosting a {{random|30-min|25-min|quick}} {{random|Lunch & Learn|live demo|webinar}} on [PRODUCT], covering [key topics] so you can {{random|streamline upcoming builds|move faster on new jobs}}.

{{random|Lunch is on us.|We'll send lunch to your team.|Food's covered, on us.}}

{{random|Does next Wednesday or Thursday afternoon suit your team?|Would next Wed or Thu afternoon work for you?}}
```

---

### Framework 3: Stat-Led Pain Opener

**What it is:** Opens with a quantified pain point, positions the solution as the fix. **Best for:** Any offer where the pain is quantifiable.

**Example:**

```
John, most eCommerce support teams spend 60%+ of their time answering the same five questions.

Our AI support tool handles all tier-1 inquiries automatically — we cut average resolution time by 70% for a similar company last quarter.

Worth a quick look?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME}, [SPECIFIC PAIN STAT about their role/industry].

{{random|Our|The}} [PRODUCT/SERVICE] {{random|handles|manages|takes care of}} [what it does to solve that] — [brief proof or mechanism].

{{random|Worth a quick look?|Want to see how it works?|Open to exploring this?}}
```

---

### Framework 4: Personalized Trigger (Enrichment-Driven)

**What it is:** Uses enrichment data (company description, keywords, services) to write a personalized opener connecting their business to the offer. **Best for:** Any industry — go-to when enrichment data is available.

**Example:**

```
John - saw that Acme Corp builds HR software for mid-market companies. Have you considered how an AI-powered onboarding tool could cut your clients' time-to-productivity in half?

We built a system that integrates with existing HRIS platforms and reduces manual onboarding tasks by 80%.
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} - {PERSONALIZATION}

{{random|Have you considered|Ever thought about|Curious if you've explored}} [how the offer connects to what they do]?

{{random|We developed|Our team built|We built}} [solution description] that [key benefit].
```

**Note:** {PERSONALIZATION} is generated from the company's keywords, products/services, and descriptions. One sentence on what they do and why the offer is relevant.

---

### Framework 5: Named Brand Case Study

**What it is:** Leads with a recognizable brand name and the result achieved for them. **Best for:** When the client has worked with recognizable brands.

**Example:**

```
John, we generated $1.2M in new pipeline in 90 days with our outbound system for HubSpot.

Interested in learning how Acme Corp can have similar results?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME}, {{random|we generated|we delivered|we drove|our team produced}} [SPECIFIC METRIC] in {{random|just|only}} [TIMEFRAME] with {{random|our|a proven|a tailored}} [service type] for [BRAND NAME].

{{random|Interested in learning how {COMPANY} can have similar results?|Want to see how {COMPANY} can achieve the same?}}
```

---

### Framework 6: Anonymized "Similar Company" Case Study

**What it is:** Describes a result for a company similar to the prospect without naming them. **Best for:** When no name-droppable brands, or confidentiality matters.

**Example:**

```
John – we recently helped an HR tech startup that doubled their qualified pipeline to 40 meetings/month while maintaining a near-100% show rate.

Would you like to learn how?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – {{random|we recently helped|we just helped|we worked with}} [similar company descriptor] that {{random|doubled|grew|scaled}} [metric] to [number] while {{random|maintaining|keeping}} [quality metric].

{{random|Would you like to learn how?|Want to see how we did it?|Interested in how we made it happen?}}
```

---

### Framework 7: Playbook / Deck Offer

**What it is:** Offers to share a playbook or case study deck built for a recognizable brand. **Best for:** Consulting, strategy, audience intelligence.

**Example:**

```
John - our team put together a playbook for Salesforce outlining how we built their entire outbound SDR process from scratch and got them to 50 meetings/month in 60 days.

Mind if I share it with you?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} - our team {{random|created|developed|put together}} a playbook for [BRAND] {{random|outlining|showing|detailing}} how we [what you did for them].

{{random|Mind if I share it with you?|Would you like to see it?|Can I send it your way?}}
```

---

### Framework 8: Custom Strategy Deck (High-Value Freebie)

**What it is:** Offers to create a custom strategy deck anchored by a big brand result. Mentions the deck's retail value. **Best for:** Agencies, influencer marketing, creative services.

**Example:**

```
John – got a minute to see the influencer strategy we used to help Nike generate $4M in attributed revenue last quarter?

We'll also build a custom strategy deck for Acme Corp showing how you can achieve similar results — completely free (companies typically pay us $2,500+ for these).

Would you like me to send it over?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – {{random|have a moment|quick sec|got a minute}} to {{random|see|check out}} the [service] strategy we used to help [BRAND] {{random|generate|drive|hit}} [BIG METRIC]?

We'll also {{random|create|put together|build}} a {{random|tailored|custom|personalized}} strategy deck for {COMPANY}, showing how you can achieve similar results — completely {{random|complimentary|free|on the house}} ({{random|companies typically pay us|usually priced at}} $[VALUE]+ for these).

{{random|Would you like me to send it over?|Open to seeing what that might look like?}}
```

---

### Framework 9: Competitive Displacement / Contract Buyout

**What it is:** Targets prospects using a specific competitor, offers to replace with a migration/buyout sweetener. **Best for:** SaaS replacement, agency switches, vendor changes.

**Example:**

```
John,

I know Salesforce's premium pricing and ongoing consulting fees can seriously drain your software budget.

We'll buy out your contract and migrate all your data and workflows over without disrupting operations — would you be open to exploring this further?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME},

{{random|I know|I understand}} [COMPETITOR]'s {{random|premium pricing and ongoing consulting fees|high costs and complexity}} can {{random|seriously drain|take a big hit on}} your [budget type].

[COMPANY NAME] will {{random|buy out|cover}} your contract and {{random|migrate your assets|transfer everything over}} without {{random|disrupting operations|causing downtime}}, so {{random|are you open to exploring this further?|would you be open to discussing this?}}
```

---

### Framework 10: Competitor Frustration + Case Study

**What it is:** Validates frustration with the incumbent, then proves the switch works with specific savings. **Best for:** Direct competitor replacement with a strong migration case study.

**Example:**

```
John,

A mid-market SaaS company cut their software costs by 60% when they switched from Salesforce to our platform.

We'll cover your remaining contract and migrate everything over without causing any downtime.

Want to see if Acme Corp could score similar results?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME},

{{random|An Accenture company|A Fortune 500 subsidiary|A company similar to yours}} {{random|slashed|cut}} their [cost type] by [X%] when they switched from [COMPETITOR] to [YOUR PRODUCT].

We'll {{random|buy out|cover}} your contract and {{random|migrate assets|transfer everything over}} without {{random|disrupting your day-to-day operations|causing any downtime}}.

{{random|Want to see if {COMPANY} could score similar results?|Curious if {COMPANY} could achieve the same savings?}}
```

---

### Framework 11: Direct Question Opener

**What it is:** Single specific question about whether they have a need, followed by a brief pitch. **Best for:** Technical or niche products where the need is binary.

**Example:**

```
John — does your team currently have a way to automatically re-engage leads that go cold after the first demo?

We built a system that identifies and re-sequences cold leads and gets them back on the calendar. Happy to show you how it works.
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} — [DIRECT QUESTION about a specific need they likely have]?

{{random|Our team developed|We developed|We built}} [solution] that [key benefit]. {{random|Would love to show you how it works|Happy to share how it works|Interested in a quick overview?}}
```

---

### Framework 12: Done-For-You System + Result

**What it is:** Pitches a fully managed system with a specific result. Emphasizes the client's team didn't touch it. **Best for:** Managed services, agencies, outsourced operations.

**Example:**

```
John - we build fully managed cold email systems. Message your entire addressable market in 90 days, go live in 2 weeks.

Built this for a fintech startup similar to yours. 47 meetings booked in the first 60 days. Their team never touched it.

Worth a quick chat?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} - we {{random|build|create}} {{random|done-for-you|fully managed}} [service description]. {{random|Message your entire TAM in 90 days|[Big promise in specific timeframe]}}, {{random|launch in|go live in}} [timeframe].

{{random|Built this for|Did this for}} [similar company]. [Specific results]. Their team {{random|didn't touch it|never touched it}}.

{{random|Open to a quick look?|Worth a quick chat?}}
```

---

### Framework 13: Pay-Per-Performance Pitch

**What it is:** Leads with performance-based pricing as the hook. **Best for:** Lead gen agencies, appointment setting, pay-per-call.

**Example:**

```
John - we book qualified sales meetings on a pay-per-meeting basis. You only pay for calls that actually show.

Did this for a cybersecurity firm — 34 meetings in the first month. Also a SaaS company in your space (22 meetings, $180k pipeline generated).

Open to a quick chat?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} - we {{random|book|schedule}} [result type] on a {{random|pay-per-call|performance|pay-per-meeting}} basis. You {{random|only pay for|just pay for}} [what actually happens].

{{random|Did this for|Built this for}} [company]. {{random|Also|Plus}} [company2] ([specific metric]).

{{random|Open to a quick chat?|Worth a quick look?}}
```

---

### Framework 14: AI/Tech Replacing Labor

**What it is:** Identifies labor/staffing pain, positions AI/tech as the replacement. **Best for:** AI agent companies, automation, chatbots.

**Example:**

```
John, most property management companies spend 20+ hours a week manually following up with maintenance requests and tenant inquiries.

Our AI assistant handles all inbound tenant communication via voice and text — no new hires needed.

We'll set up a free trial for Acme Property Group. Want to test it?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME}, [labor pain statement for their industry].

Our {{random|AI agent|AI assistant|automated system}} {{random|handles|manages|takes care of}} [specific tasks], {{random|no new hires needed|without hiring anyone|no extra staffing required}}.

We'll {{random|set up|launch|start}} a free trial for {COMPANY}. {{random|Want to test it?|Interested in giving it a try?}}
```

---

### Framework 15: "Already Built This For You"

**What it is:** States you've already created something for their company before they asked. **Best for:** AI products, creative agencies, anything with pre-buildable demos.

**Example:**

```
John – built a custom AI chatbot for Acme Corp that acts as a 24/7 sales assistant on your website — can I share a 60-second video showing you how it works?

I noticed your site gets significant traffic but has no way to capture and qualify leads after hours.

P.S. We built something similar for a SaaS company in your space — they booked 14 demos in the first month without touching it.
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – {{random|created|built|put together}} a [deliverable] for {COMPANY} that {{random|acts as|serves as|functions as}} [what it does] — {{random|can I share|mind if I send over}} a 60-second video {{random|showing you how it works?|that walks you through it?}}

[1 sentence on why it's relevant to them specifically].

P.S. [PROOF STAT from similar company]
```

---

### Framework 16: Personalized Campaign Ideas (Bullets)

**What it is:** Shares 2-3 specific campaign ideas tailored to their brand. **Best for:** Influencer marketing, creative, social, content marketing. One of the few frameworks where bullet points in the body are acceptable.

**Example:**

```
John – had a few influencer campaign ideas for Acme Corp I wanted to run by you:

- A micro-influencer seeding campaign targeting fitness creators with 50k–200k followers in your core demographic
- A UGC contest to generate authentic content ahead of your Q3 product launch
- A brand ambassador program with 5 anchor creators for consistent monthly exposure

Is influencer marketing a priority this year?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – {{random|had a few|thought of some|came up with a few}} [campaign type] ideas for {COMPANY}:

- {IDEA 1}
- {IDEA 2}
- {IDEA 3}

{{random|Is [campaign type] a priority this year?|Would you consider [campaign type] a key focus this year?}}
```

---

### Framework 17: Data / Research Insight Lead

**What it is:** Leads with proprietary data or research your team conducted. **Best for:** Agencies with data, research firms, analytics, audience intelligence.

**Example:**

```
John – looking for deeper insights on what enterprise buyers actually prioritize in HR software?

Our analysis of 2.3M LinkedIn posts and job listings revealed a major shift toward employee experience tools over traditional compliance platforms — directly in line with what Acme Corp offers.

Want to be first to act on these trends?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – {{random|looking for|interested in|curious about}} deeper insights on [their industry] behavior?

Our {{random|analysis|breakdown|study}} of [DATASET SIZE] [data source] {{random|revealed|highlighted|uncovered}} shifting preferences toward [relevant trend] like those in {COMPANY}'s {{random|portfolio|lineup|newest offerings}}.

{{random|Want to be first to act on these trends?|Should we talk about how {COMPANY} can align with these shifts?}}
```

---

### Framework 18: Content Repurposing Angle

**What it is:** Asks if they're fully leveraging existing content across channels. **Best for:** Content agencies, video production, social media management.

**Example:**

```
John – is Acme Corp fully leveraging your existing webinar content across marketing channels?

We helped Gartner repurpose their quarterly research reports into 90+ short-form videos, driving 4M organic views and 2,400 inbound leads in one quarter.

Interested in seeing how this could work for Acme Corp?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – is {COMPANY} {{random|fully leveraging|maximizing|making the most of}} [content type] across {{random|marketing channels|all marketing touchpoints|digital channels}}?

We've {{random|helped|worked with|partnered with}} [brand] repurpose [content type] into [output], {{random|get|drive|generate}} [big result], and [business outcome].

{{random|Interested in seeing how this could work for {COMPANY}?|Worth exploring for {COMPANY}?}}
```

---

### Framework 19: Trend / News / Policy Hook

**What it is:** Leads with a recent industry change, then positions the offer as the response. **Best for:** Offers tied to regulatory changes, platform updates, market shifts. Time-sensitive — these scripts have a shelf life.

**Example:**

```
John – the FTC just updated their disclosure rules to require explicit AI labeling on all AI-generated marketing content.

We've helped 12 marketing agencies navigate this transition, averaging a 3-week turnaround to full compliance without pausing campaigns.

Ready to get ahead of this before your competitors scramble to catch up?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – [PLATFORM/REGULATOR] just {{random|updated their policy|changed their rules|rolled out a new update}} to {{random|allow|require}} [relevant action for their business].

We've {{random|helped|worked with|guided}} [similar companies] {{random|navigate|take advantage of|capitalize on}} this, {{random|averaging|seeing|with}} [proof point].

{{random|Ready to establish your presence before competitors?|Want to move early before others catch on?}}
```

---

### Framework 20: Urgency / Seasonal Window

**What it is:** Creates time pressure around seasonal cycles, limited capacity, or price increases. **Best for:** Seasonal services, campaigns, known price fluctuations.

**Example:**

```
John – is Acme Corp locking in HVAC service contracts before summer pricing spikes?

Demand from commercial property managers typically increases 40% between May and August, and contractor availability tightens fast.

Want to lock in now before the rush hits?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – is {COMPANY} {{random|securing|locking in|booking}} [seasonal opportunity] before {{random|rates increase|prices spike|costs go up|the rush hits}}?

[Market data supporting urgency].

{{random|Ready to secure early?|Want to lock in now?|Should we help {COMPANY} get ahead of the rush?}}
```

---

### Framework 21: Missing Feature / Gap Spotted

**What it is:** Points out something missing from their website or process, offers to fix it. **Best for:** Web design, SaaS, compliance.

**Example:**

```
Hey John, came across Acme Corp's website and noticed you don't have a lead capture form on your pricing page — figured I'd reach out.

We've added lead capture systems to 60+ B2B SaaS sites, averaging a 23% increase in demo requests within the first 30 days.

I put together 3 mockups for Acme Corp — want me to send them your way?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{{random|Hi|Hey}} {FIRST_NAME}, {{random|came across your website and noticed|was checking out {COMPANY} and couldn't find}} a [MISSING FEATURE] – {{random|figured I'd reach out|thought I'd send a quick note}}.

[Credential/social proof for similar companies].

{{random|I put together|I mocked up}} [NUMBER] {{random|examples|mockups|ideas}} for {COMPANY} – {{random|want me to send them your way?|let me know if you'd like a look}}.
```

---

### Framework 22: Free Mockup / Visual Deliverable

**What it is:** Offers free mockups, designs, or samples specific to their business. **Best for:** Web design, branding, physical products.

**Example:**

```
Hey John, noticed Acme Corp's packaging design doesn't quite match your new brand direction.

We've rebranded packaging for 30+ DTC brands this past year.

Want me to send a free custom mockup for Acme Corp?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{{random|Hey|Hi}} {FIRST_NAME}, [observation about their current state or gap].

[Brief credential].

Want me to send a free {{random|branded|custom}} {{random|design|mockup|sample|concept}} for {COMPANY}?
```

---

### Framework 23: Free Trial / Pilot Offer

**What it is:** Offers a bounded free trial or pilot with specific parameters. **Best for:** SaaS, email outbound, AI tools, testable services.

**Example:**

```
John - looks like Acme Corp manages a large field service team across multiple territories.

Most field service companies still manually dispatch jobs and chase down technician status updates. We automate the entire dispatch and communication workflow.

Worth testing with a free 2-week pilot for Acme Corp?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} - {{random|noticed|saw|looks like}} {COMPANY} {{random|coordinates|runs|manages}} [relevant activity].

{{random|Most|Many|Nearly all}} [their industry] teams {{random|manually chase|spend hours on|still manually handle}} [pain point]. We {{random|automate the entire|handle all the}} [process].

{{random|Worth testing with|Open to|Interested in}} a {{random|free|no-cost}} [pilot parameters] for {COMPANY}?
```

---

### Framework 24: TAM / Market Size Hook

**What it is:** Quantifies their total addressable market and shows how many potential customers they could reach. **Best for:** Outbound agencies, lead gen, data providers.

**Example:**

```
John – looking to reach CFOs at mid-market manufacturing companies directly?

There are approximately 14,000 of these decision-makers in the US and you can reach 90% of them through targeted outbound email.

Interested in a free campaign to see how this could work for Acme Corp?

P.S. We specialize in building outbound systems that reach our client's full TAM every 30-90 days.
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} – looking to reach [ICP] directly?

There's approximately [NUMBER] of these people in [GEOGRAPHY] & you can reach {{random|90%|the vast majority|almost all}} of them {{random|sending emails like these|through outbound|via targeted outreach}}.

Interested in a {{random|free campaign|free test|quick pilot}} to see how this could work for {COMPANY}?

P.S. We specialize in building email outbound systems that reach our client's full TAM every 30-90 days.
```

---

### Framework 25: LinkedIn / Social Engagement Trigger

**What it is:** References their recent LinkedIn activity as the reason for outreach. **Best for:** Any offer when LinkedIn activity data is available.

**Example:**

```
John - it's Mike from Outreach Co. Saw you engaged with Gary Vee's recent post about brand authenticity and wanted to reach out.

Checked out Acme Corp and came up with a few UGC campaign ideas that could be valuable:

- A customer story series for LinkedIn featuring real enterprise clients
- A behind-the-scenes video campaign showing your team's creative process
- A thought leadership series positioning your CEO in the HR tech space

I'd love to build out one of these for you at no cost. Would you be interested?
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{FIRST_NAME} - it's {{random|Sender}} from [Company]. Saw {{random|you engaged with|your comment on}} [PERSON]'s recent [post topic] and {{random|thought|wanted}} to reach out.

{{random|Took a look at|Checked out}} your {{random|profile|company}} & {{random|came up with|had}} a few [use case type] that could be valuable:

- {BP1}
- {BP2}
- {BP3}

I'd love to build out one of these for you {{random|at no cost|free of charge}}. {{random|Would you be interested?|Open to it?}}
```

---

## Follow-Up Frameworks

### Framework 26: "Right Person?" Thread Reply

**What it is:** Ultra-short routing question. Under 25 words. Always a thread reply. **Best for:** Universal follow-up on any Email 1.

**Example:**

```
Am I reaching out to the right person to chat about outbound lead generation at Acme Corp, John?

- Mike
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
Am I {{random|reaching out to|contacting|getting in touch with}} the right person to {{random|chat|talk|discuss}} about [TOPIC] {{random|at|for}} {COMPANY}, {FIRST_NAME}?

- {{random|Sender|Sender L.|Full Name}}
```

---

### Framework 27: Warm Follow-Up / Float Back Up

**What it is:** Gentle nudge. Acknowledges they're busy. Zero pressure. **Best for:** Follow-up when Email 1 got no response.

**Example:**

```
Floating this back up, John.

If outbound isn't a priority right now, no worries. But if it's worth exploring, happy to show you what we built for a similar SaaS company in your space.

Either way,
Mike
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{{random|Floating this back up|Just following up}}, {FIRST_NAME}.

{{random|If [topic] isn't a priority right now, no worries.|If this isn't on your radar right now, totally understand.}} {{random|But if it is|If it's worth exploring}}, {{random|happy to show you what we built|can show you how this works}} for [similar company/use case].

{{random|Either way|No pressure}},
{{random|Sender|Sender L.|Full Name}}
```

---

### Framework 28: Soft Breakup

**What it is:** Acknowledges silence, gives easy out, keeps door open. **Best for:** Clean, respectful final touch.

**Example:**

```
Hey John,

Was hoping to hear from you — is there anything holding you back from exploring our outbound system for Acme Corp?

- Mike

P.S. If my emails aren't your thing, just let me know — no worries at all.
```

**Template (with spintax):**

```
{{random|Hey|Hi}} {FIRST_NAME},

Was {{random|hoping|wanting}} to hear from you - is there {{random|anything|something}} holding you back from exploring [offer] for {COMPANY}?

- {{random|Sender|Sender L.|Full Name}}

P.S. If my {{random|emails|messages}} aren't {{random|your thing|of interest}}, just let me know - no worries at all.
```

---

## CTA Templates

**CTA A — Show Exactly How**

```
On a {{random|short call|brief call|quick intro}}, I'll {{random|show you exactly|walk you through|break down}} what we're doing for [business_type] to [result] - even if you {{random|want to take the strategies and run with them yourself|end up keeping things in-house|just want the strategy}}.
```

**CTA B — Break Down + Freebie**

```
On a {{random|brief call|short call|quick intro}}, I'll {{random|break down|walk you through}} {{random|how we build out prospect lists|what strategy we use and the full approach}} - even if you {{random|end up doing it in-house|decide to handle it yourself}}. We'll also {{random|put together|send over}} a list of [NUMBER] targeted contacts {{random|just for hopping on|just for the time}}.
```

**CTA C — Worth a Quick Look**  
`{{random|Worth a quick look?|Open to learning more?|Curious to see how it works?}}`

**CTA D — Mind If I Share**  
`{{random|Mind if I share it with you?|Would you like me to send it over?|Can I send it your way?}}`

**CTA E — Want to See Similar Results**  
`{{random|Want to see if {COMPANY} could score similar results?|Curious if {COMPANY} could achieve the same?}}`

**CTA F — Binary Question**  
`{{random|Would it be worth a quick chat?|Open to a quick call?|Want to see how we did it?}}`

---

## Subject Line Templates

```
{{random|keyword1|keyword2|keyword3}}
{{random|for {COMPANY}|about {COMPANY}|{COMPANY} playbook}}
{{random|question for {COMPANY}|about {COMPANY}|question {FIRST_NAME}}}
{FIRST_NAME}, {{random|seen this?|quick question|thought of you}}
{{random|About {COMPANY}|Idea for {COMPANY}|{COMPANY} + 3 quick ideas}}
```

For thread reply follow-ups, prefix with `Re:` on the original subject.

---

## Principles From Top Performers

1. **Specificity wins.** "$200k/month from $0 in 90 days" beats "significant revenue growth."
2. **CTA must be binary.** "Worth a quick look?" not "What are your thoughts?"
3. **Social proof > claims.** Don't say "we're the best." Say "we did Y for Z."
4. **Short > long.** Highest performers almost all under 50 words.
5. **3-4 spintax variants** per bracket for deliverability.
6. **Domain obfuscation** in signatures: `company(dot)com`
7. **P.S. lines** work for proof stats that would clutter the body.
8. **"Even if you do it in-house"** is a top CTA pattern.
9. **Name specific ICP titles** — not "decision makers" but "VPs of Marketing at mid-market SaaS."
10. **Multiple case studies > one.** Drop 2 names with results when possible.

✍️ Using AI to Write Personalized & Bespoke Emails

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Placeholder Video — Coming Soon.**

---

## What is a Cold Email?

A cold email is an unsolicited email sent to a prospect without prior contact. Like cold calling, it's a method of starting a conversation with potential clients — but done well, it creates visibility, builds interest, and gets people to take action.

### The Ever-Evolving Landscape

Cold email has changed significantly. The days of blasting generic templates and getting results are over. Today, landing in the inbox requires the right infrastructure, and getting a reply requires the right message. The fundamentals below are what actually move the needle.

---

## Understanding the Fundamentals

### 1. Relevance

Relevance is the most overlooked fundamental. Your email needs to be written for the specific person or type of person you're reaching out to — not a generic blast that could apply to anyone. If it doesn't feel relevant to the reader, it gets deleted.

### 2. Length

**Keep it short. 40–80 words is the target.** Response rates drop significantly with every additional sentence. Hit your main point in 2–3 sentences. If they're interested, they'll reply — you don't need to sell everything in the first email.

### 3. Subject Lines

Your subject line is the first thing a prospect sees and determines whether the email gets opened or ignored. Best practices:

- Keep it short and conversational — don't write a headline
- Using just the **first name variable** as the subject line has produced some of the best deliverability results we've seen — first names are effectively infinite, so every send is different
- Avoid spam trigger words, excessive punctuation, and anything that looks like marketing
- Test subject lines regularly

### 4. Focus on Outcome and Offer

Lead with what the prospect gets — not what you do. Nobody cares about your process until they care about the result. The offer is the most important part of the email.

### 5. Personalization

Even light personalization dramatically improves reply rates. At minimum, make the email feel like it was written for this type of person. The more specific and relevant it feels, the better.

### 6. Call to Action

The best CTAs are ones where you're **offering something** rather than just asking for a call. Offering a free deliverable (a report, an audit, a sample list, a Loom video, etc.) consistently outperforms straight call requests. If you're going to ask for a call, **pair it with a lead magnet**.

*"If you'd be open to a quick intro call, we'll walk you through exactly how the system works — and we'll put together a free list of 200 leads your team could start working, just for taking the time to connect."*

### 7. Testing and Optimization

Run at least 2–3 campaign variations at a time so you're always learning. Change one variable at a time — offer, structure, subject line — so you know what's actually driving results.

---

## Cold Email Structure

**1. Subject Line** — Short, conversational, relevant. First name variable is a strong default.

**2. First Sentence (Opener)** — Lead with relevance. Something specific to them or their situation that shows this isn't a mass blast.

**3. Email Body (Offer + Mechanism)** — State what you do, what result it creates, and briefly explain how. Keep it to 1–2 sentences max.

**4. CTA** — One clear ask. Ideally a free deliverable or a call offer paired with a lead magnet. Don't give them multiple options.

---

## Frameworks and Approaches

### Fibonacci Technique

Start short and simple. If they don't reply, your follow-up is slightly longer and adds a new angle. Each step builds on the last.

### REPLY Framework

- **R** — Relevance
- **E** — Empathy
- **P** — Problem
- **L** — Lead-in
- **Y** — Your ask

### Scenario-Based Approach

Write the email around a specific scenario the prospect is likely experiencing. "If you're anything like most [job title]s we speak to, you're probably dealing with [pain point]..." — it makes the email feel personal without requiring deep research on every lead.

### Reporting Old Results

If you have case studies or proof points, lead with the result: "We helped [type of company] go from X to Y in Z timeframe." Social proof in the opener significantly boosts credibility.

---

## Common Mistakes To Avoid

**Writing From Templates** — Generic templates are easy to fingerprint and easy to ignore. Use frameworks, not fill-in-the-blank templates.

**Ignoring Personalization** — Even one personalized line changes how the email reads. Mass blasts with zero personalization tank reply rates.

**Leading With Your Product** — Nobody cares what your product is before they care about the result it creates. Lead with outcome, not features.

**Overcomplicating the Email** — More words, more sentences, more context — less replies. Keep it tight.

**Skipping Testing** — If you're not running multiple variations, you have no idea what's actually working. Always be testing.

**Neglecting Follow-Ups** — Most replies come on follow-up emails. A 2–3 step sequence is the minimum. Don't send one email and give up.

---

## Spintax

Spintax lets you rotate words and phrases across emails so each send looks slightly different to email service providers (ESPs).

⚠️ **Warning:** **Important:** ESPs are now fingerprinting at the structural level — not just the words. Rotating synonyms through spintax isn't enough if the structure of the email stays the same. You need to periodically change the **length and layout** of the email too. If your current email is opener → body → CTA, try switching to opener → CTA only. If it's 3 lines, try 5. The goal is to make each send look structurally different enough that it can't be fingerprinted as the same campaign.
