cold-email/presenting-offer.content:
# Presenting Your Offer in a Cold Email

The purpose of this section is to help you present your offer in your messaging and stand out from everyone else selling lead generation.

How To Stand Out From Everyone Else Selling Lead Gen

The thing with selling lead gen is that many companies have received so much outreach over the years that they may instantly class your outreach as "one of those." The problem isn't that the market is saturated or lead gen is "dead" — it's that we need to find more innovative ways of differentiating our services so we can crack the market.

### How To Stand Out

**Video:** [Watch on Loom](https://www.loom.com/share/5169c4af09ae43f7a8105fb09c2cb542)

The "Detailed Strategy" Angle

This is what we call the **"Detailed Strategy" angle**. This angle is used in your email and then your template document would be amended when a prospect replies that seems to match your ICP. This way you're only doing the work when needed and the speed at which you can amend it means it was already pre-done for them.

### Email Template

**Subject: Exclusive Outreach Strategy for {companyname}**

```
Hi {firstname},

We've been studying {companyname}'s unique positioning in the market and believe there's a significant opportunity for you to leverage. As a result, we've crafted a bespoke outreach strategy tailored specifically to {companyname} to help you achieve {your outcome}.

Would you mind if I sent this to you?

Thanks,
{Name}
```

### 'Detailed Outreach Strategy' Template

**Title: Detailed Outreach Strategy for {companyname}**

**Introduction:** At [Your Agency Name], we pride ourselves on crafting unique strategies that align with a company's strengths. For {companyname}, we've meticulously analyzed your case studies and are confident in the plan we've laid out below.

**1. Leveraging {companyname} Case Studies:**

- Case Study 1: {CaseStudy1Title}
- Case Study 2: {CaseStudy2Title}
- Case Study 3: {CaseStudy3Title}

These case studies will act as the foundation of our outreach, demonstrating the tangible results {companyname} has delivered in the past.

**2. Targeting Companies with Similar Profiles:** By identifying businesses that resemble those from your case studies, we can present them with relevant success stories, making the proposition more compelling.

**3. Campaign Blueprint:**  
Total Addressable Market: {TotalLeadsFound} companies identified for outreach  
Emails to be Sent: {NumberOfEmailsPlanned}  
Domains Used for Sending: {Domain1}, {Domain2}, {Domain3}, {Domain4}, {Domain5}, etc.

**Performance Metrics:** Based on our historical data and industry benchmarks:

| Metric | Our Averages | Industry |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Open Rate | 82% | {IndustryOpenRate} |
| Reply Rate | 12% | {IndustryReplyRate} |
| Meeting Booked Rate | 1.8% | {IndustryMeetingRate} |

**4. Conclusion:** We're excited about the potential results this strategy can deliver for {companyname}. Our approach is backed by data, tailored to your strengths, and designed to maximize engagement and conversions. To discuss this strategy further, please Book a Call with our team.

cold-email/reply-management.title: Reply Management
cold-email/reply-management.content:
# Reply Management

⚠️ **Warning:** The most important thing in reply management is **responding to leads within 5 minutes.** Set up webhooks from your sequencer to Slack for interested replies only, so you get notified instantly.

**Booking calls:**

- Never send a Calendly link
- Always suggest two specific times — "Is Monday at 3pm or Tuesday at 4pm better for you?"
- Get a written agreement from the prospect for a specific time
- Manually book them in after they confirm

**Sales assets:** Know how to send sales assets to people who want more information, and always follow up with them if they go quiet after receiving them.

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Placeholder Video — Reply Management Process & Booking Calls**

---

## The Goal of Every Reply

The point of reply management is always to move the sale forward. Every reply you send should have one objective: **get them on a call.** Never let a reply sit without a clear next step attached. If you're answering a question, answer it briefly and immediately redirect to scheduling. The conversation lives on the call — not in the inbox.

---

## Common Reply Scenarios

### "What's the price?"

Two mistakes people make: giving an exact price (too early, kills momentum) or being completely vague (frustrating, sounds evasive). The right approach: tell them you typically start with a short paid trial that functions as a proof of concept — give a fixed fee range for that. Then let them know that ongoing work varies depending on scope.

*"Great question — we typically start with a short paid trial that acts as a proof of concept, usually in the range of $X–$X. From there, ongoing work is variable depending on scope, but most clients end up in the $X–$X/month range. Happy to walk you through exactly what that looks like — is [Day] at [Time] or [Day] at [Time] better for a quick call?"*

Always give a range, never an exact number. Acknowledge the trial/POC structure first — it lowers perceived commitment. Always end with a push to a call with two suggested times.

### "Can you send me more information?"

Send a short one-pager or deck if you have one, but keep your message brief. Don't write a novel in the reply. Follow up within 24–48 hours if they go quiet.

*"Of course — I'll send that over now. In the meantime, would it make sense to jump on a quick call so I can tailor it to what's most relevant for [Company]? [Day] at [Time] or [Day] at [Time] — what works better?"*

### "Not interested"

Don't push. A short, gracious reply keeps the door open.

*"Totally understood — appreciate you getting back to me. I'll leave it there. If anything changes down the road, feel free to reach out."*

### "Who is this?" / "How did you get my info?"

Be transparent and direct. Don't over-apologize — just answer briefly and re-engage.

*"Hey [Name] — [Your name] here. Found your info through [LinkedIn/Apollo/etc.] and reached out because [one-line reason]. Happy to take you off our list if you'd prefer — but if [outcome] is something on your radar, might be worth a 15-minute call. [Day] or [Day] work?"*

### "Call me" / "Give me a call"

This is a green light. Respond immediately, confirm a specific time, and manually put it in your calendar.

*"Perfect — would [Day] at [Time] or [Day] at [Time] work? I'll send a calendar invite once we confirm."*

---

## The Two-Time Rule

Always suggest **two specific times** — never just "let me know when you're free" and never just a Calendly link. Two options creates a binary choice that's easy to respond to and keeps the momentum going.

- ✅ "Is Monday at 3pm or Tuesday at 4pm better for you?"
- ❌ "Feel free to book a time on my calendar here: [link]"
- ❌ "Let me know when works for you"

Once they confirm a time, manually add it to your calendar and send a confirmation. This also gives you a natural touchpoint to send any pre-call materials.

# Running + Improving Campaigns

How to judge your campaign metrics and optimize. Campaigns will have a lot of variants. After a campaign has been running for a while, go into the campaign, look at what variants are performing the best, and use that to write more.

📊 Market Research and Targeting

⚠️ **Warning:** **THE #1 AREA WHERE MOST PEOPLE CAN INSTANTLY IMPROVE THEIR CAMPAIGNS IS THROUGH BETTER UNDERSTANDING OF THE MARKET AND THEIR TARGET TITLES.** Market research and understanding your target market is the single biggest difference between getting good or bad email results.

### How To Use ChatGPT To Do Market Research For You

We're going to use GO Lead Generation as the example company — a lead generation agency targeting B2B SaaS companies in the US.

**Step 1 — Identify Target Industry:**

```
We want to conduct market research on B2B SaaS companies in order to better sell them our Lead Generation Services. Our ICP is B2B SaaS companies, located in the USA, with at least 10 employees and doing over $1 million a year. Please respond "OK" to confirm you have understood.
```

**Step 2 — Identify Job Titles:**

```
Identify the job titles responsible for generating leads, booking new client meetings and growing revenue within B2B SaaS companies.
```

**Step 3 — Finding Decision Makers:**

```
Determine the roles that would most likely have the authority to make a decision on working with us.
```

**Step 4 — Identifying Pain Points:**

```
Create a table where column 1 lists the job titles of the decision-makers. Create a new column and please list the common pain points faced by each job title as it relates to [Your Service's Outcome].
```

| Job Title | Common Pain Points |
| --- | --- |
| CEO / Founder | Aligning sales and marketing with overall business strategy; ensuring sustainable growth; balancing investment in lead generation with other priorities |
| Chief Revenue Officer | Maximizing revenue across all channels; integrating sales and marketing for better ROI; finding and retaining high-quality leads that convert |
| VP of Sales | Meeting sales targets and managing the sales pipeline; enhancing team productivity; adapting to evolving market demands |
| CMO / Marketing Director | Generating high-quality leads for the sales team; measuring and improving ROI of marketing campaigns; keeping up with changing trends |
| Director of Business Development | Identifying and pursuing new market opportunities; building and maintaining strategic partnerships; balancing short-term and long-term goals |

**Step 5 — Rank Pain Points by Impact (1-10):**

```
In column 2 please rank these pain points based on their likely impact on the relevant job titles from 1-10. 1 being very low impact and 10 being extremely high.
```

**Step 6 — Understanding Solutions Previously Tried:**

```
In a fourth column, list the typical solutions each job title will have tried to overcome the pain points in column 2.
```

**Step 7 — Using Research to Provide Solutions:**

```
Here's [your company's] website text. Please create a fifth column that outlines how we can help the relevant job titles from column 1 solve the pain points in column 2.
```

Use the research above to augment your email copy and data points so you can speak directly to your prospect's world.

---

### Other Ways To Conduct Market Research

- **Surveys and questionnaires:** In exchange for a small credit or incentive
- **Interviews with industry experts:** Reach out to prospects and ask if you can interview them — they often share pain points, priorities, and how they think about buying
- **Social media and forums:** Reddit, LinkedIn, Slack communities, Facebook groups in your niche
- **Customer feedback:** Monitor reviews of your target niche's existing vendors and tools

📤 Running Outreach

⚠️ **Warning:** Make sure to go through **everything** on the Manage Deliverability page before running outreach.

### Key Metrics To Track Campaign Performance

| Metric | Target |
| --- | --- |
| Reply rate | 5% |
| Overall positive reply rate | 2% |
| Positive reply as % of replies | 40% |
| Meeting booked rate | 1.8% (industry average 0.5–1%) |
| Positive reply to meeting conversion rate | 45% |
| Call show-up rate | 80% |
| Bounce rate | <2% |

### Campaign Performance Tracking Spreadsheet

💡 [Access the Outreach Performance Spreadsheet](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1QyiPvUeIOFZmjw_n09ntMIG-weX6czuiMymglQomWo0/edit)

The spreadsheet tracks Calls Booked, Calls Showed, Show Rate %, Clients Closed, and Closure Rate %. Use it to determine optimal email volume, identify conversion trends, and monitor campaign health.

### Review and Adjust Regularly

The key to successful campaign management is not just in tracking but in regularly reviewing the data and making necessary adjustments. Look at what variants are performing the best and use that to write more.

---

[📄
Campaign FAQs](#)

cold-email/campaign-faqs.title: Campaign FAQs
cold-email/campaign-faqs.content:
# Campaign FAQs

Should I track open rates?

We recommend **not** tracking opens. Track performance using the reply rate instead.

Use this checklist to identify what the issue is:

1. Subject Line
2. Sending Domain
3. Sending Volume
4. Sending Times
5. Email List Cleanliness
6. Targeting
7. DKIM, SPF, and DMARC Records
8. Workspace Settings
9. A/B Testing of Subject Lines
10. A/B Testing of Subject Lines and Sending Times

First lines — worth it?

Generally — No. But scrape 1000 leads for your first campaign. Split it into 2 batches of 500 leads. One with first-lines and one without. Compare the results and see if it's worth adding first-lines to justify the cost.

What are some effective subject lines for increasing open rates?

For effective subject lines, you should:

- Keep them short and intriguing
- Use personalised and relevant information
- Create a sense of urgency or exclusivity
- A/B test different subject lines

**Try out these subject lines:**

- quick question (first name)
- quick question (company name)
- question about (company name)
- (your company name) + (their company name)
- {firstname}, 10 calls with your ideal clients on us
- Does (company name) need more leads?
- struggling with churn rate?

What are some A/B testing strategies for improving email performance?

For A/B testing:

- Test one variable at a time like subject lines, send times, or CTA buttons
- Use a significant sample size
- Analyze results based on key metrics like open rates, click-through rates, and conversion rates

How do I handle unsubscribes and opt-outs in email campaigns?

- Include a clear and easy-to-find unsubscribe link in every email
- Promptly honour unsubscribe requests
- Use opt-out information to refine your email strategy and targeting

What are the key metrics to measure the success of email outreach?

Key metrics for email outreach are:

- Reply Rate
- Bounce Rate
- Positive Reply Rate
- Meetings Booked Per Emails Sent

How can I reduce the chances of my emails being marked as spam?

Follow our resources and you'll be fine — make sure you've gone through the Writing Emails section thoroughly.

What should my email signature look like?

**Your name**  
Position | Company  
Phone *(not needed)*

Should I send to personal email addresses?

Ideally, no. However, in certain industries like local businesses, it can be challenging to avoid sending to personal email addresses.

Keep in mind, though, that this approach may accelerate inbox burnout.

Should I contact multiple decision-makers from the same company?

Yes, if the business has more than 25 employees.

Should I manually schedule calls or use a calendar link?

You will see a much higher call conversion rate if you manually reply to prospects to arrange calls as opposed to sending a calendar link.

As soon as you get an interested reply:

- **Don't** send a Calendly link
- **Don't** go back and forth trying to pick a date and time
- Suggest two specific times and get a written confirmation — then manually book it in

This approach consistently outperforms sending a booking link.

cold-email/lead-nurturing.title: Lead Nurturing
cold-email/lead-nurturing.content:
# Lead Nurturing

Going back to people who were interested but didn't book a call right away, or wanted more information. How to go back to them days or weeks later with another relevant touchpoint to make contact again.

🚧 **Coming Soon:** **Placeholder Video — Re-Engaging Leads in a New Cold Email Sequence.** How to take previous months' interested leads that didn't book a call and run them through another campaign in the sequencer.

---

## 5 Email Nurturing Sequence for Lead Generation Services

Below is a full 5-email nurturing sequence you can adapt for your service. Each email builds on the last, educating the prospect and warming them toward a call.

Email 1 — Introduce the Concept

**Subject:** {contact.company\_name}'s loyalty program

```
Hi {contact.first_name},

My name's {Name} from {Company Name}. We help brands collect GDPR compliant, 1st party data, direct from end consumers using our powerful loyalty platform.

Loyalty programs are systems set up by companies to elevate customer engagement, improve retention and increase customer lifetime value. It is a marketing strategy that aims to develop a stronger relationship between a customer and a brand with the objectives of repeat sales, increasing profits, and brand equity.

It's well known that it costs 5-10x more to acquire a new customer than to retain an existing one. Loyalty programs are great at pushing the customer up the loyalty ladder toward advocacy.

I have attached a more detailed resource for you to review.

If this is something you'd be interested in implementing for {contact.company_name}, happy to arrange a quick chat.

Kind regards,
{Name}
```

*Attach supportive material to the email if you have it such as decks, brochures, guides, etc.*

Email 2 — Provide Value (7 Ways)

**Subject:** 7 ways to increase {contact.company\_name}'s customer loyalty

```
Hi {contact.first_name},

Here are 7 ways to increase customer loyalty to {contact.company_name}:

1 - Understanding customers: You need to know what customers value and what they need in order to build a relationship with them.
2 - Communication: To build a relationship with a customer you need personalised communications.
3 - Engagement: To be engaged, customers need to be entertained by your content.
4 - Feedback: Reviews, continued analysis, developing new products and conflict handling.
5 - Customer service: Bad customer service can turn away a customer from a trusted brand forever.
6 - Brand identity: Determine what your brand stands for and what value you provide for customers.
7 - Loyalty program: Track the progress of customers and their transactions.

If you'd like to know more, please feel free to let me know.

Kind regards,
{Name}
```

Email 3 — Deepen the Value (6 Things)

**Subject:** 6 things a good loyalty program does

```
Hi {contact.first_name},

In my last email, I went over 7 ways to increase customer loyalty to {contact.company_name}. I'd now like to share with you 6 things a good loyalty program should do:

1 - Encourage repeat customers
2 - Create a bond with customers
3 - Go beyond transactions
4 - Turn data into insights
5 - Allow personalised actions
6 - Be flexible

If you're interested in understanding how our loyalty platform can do this for {contact.company_name}, I would love to arrange a time to have a quick chat.

Kind regards,
{Name}
```

Email 4 — More Value (Top 8 Ways to Reward)

**Subject:** Top 8 ways to reward loyal customers

```
Hi {contact.first_name},

Rewarding loyal customers is a great way to build brand loyalty and turn customers into referrers.

Here are the top 8 ways to reward your loyal customers:

1 - Offer unique access
2 - Gamified rewards
3 - Loyalty tiers
4 - Partnership rewards
5 - Referral programs
6 - Reward customer data
7 - Make them the star
8 - A surprise with an order

Are these things you'd like to implement for {contact.company_name}'s customers?

Kind regards,
{Name}
```

Email 5 — The Ask

**Subject:** {contact.company\_name} + {Company Name}

```
Hi {contact.first_name},

Over the past couple of months, I have highlighted the importance of having a loyalty program and how {Company Name} can implement such a program for {contact.company_name}.

Having recently created bespoke loyalty programs for — {List of clients worked with} — I would love to talk about how we could do this for {contact.company_name} too.

Do you have 15 minutes this week or next to discuss this further?

Kind regards,
{Name}
```

# Warm Calling

If someone showed interest over email but didn't book a call right away, you can call them directly to follow up while the conversation is still warm.

**Lead Formatter now has a LeadMagic API enrichment built in.** Take the interested lead's email address or LinkedIn profile URL and run it through LeadMagic inside Lead Formatter to pull a phone number. From there, you can call them directly.

Then suggest two specific times and book them in manually.

cold-email/cold-traffic-offer-check.title: Do You Have a Cold Traffic Offer?
cold-email/cold-traffic-offer-check.content:
# Do You Have a Cold Traffic Offer?

⚠️ **Warning:** **Read This Before You Write a Single Email.** This is the most important page in the entire Cold Email section. Most people skip straight to writing copy, building lists, and setting up infrastructure — and then wonder why they're not getting results. Nine times out of ten, the problem isn't the copy. It's not the deliverability. It's not the list. It's **the offer.** Before you touch anything else, you need to make sure you have a cold traffic offer — not a warm one.

---

## The Core Distinction: Warm Offer vs. Cold Traffic Offer

Most people come into cold email with a warm offer — an offer that only converts when the prospect already trusts you, already understands the problem, and already believes you can solve it. Warm offers work in referral and inbound. They die in cold inboxes.

A cold traffic offer works on a stranger. The prospect can say yes without:

- Trusting you
- Understanding how your mechanism works
- Believing you're good at what you do
- Being emotionally ready to commit to a full engagement

If the offer requires any of those four things to convert, it's a warm offer. It needs to be reframed before it goes into cold email.

---

## The Four Tests of a Cold Traffic Offer

Run your offer through these before writing a single word of copy. If it fails any of them, reframe first.

### Test 1 — Specific Numeric Outcome

There is a real number attached to the result. Not "more leads" — "8-12 qualified meetings per month." Not "grow revenue" — "add $100K in net margin in 5 months." The number can be a range or anchored to a case study. But it has to exist.

### Test 2 — Finite Timeframe and Scope

The offer has a defined end. "90-day pipeline accelerator." "First 60 days." Open-ended offers — "ongoing partnership," "retainer engagement," "we'll be your growth team" — fail this test. The prospect needs to be able to see the bottom of the commitment.

### Test 3 — New Money or Opportunity, Not Optimization

The offer produces something that doesn't exist yet — new revenue, new pipeline, new margin the business isn't currently capturing. Optimization offers fail because they require the prospect to already believe there's a problem worth fixing. That's a warm-audience assumption.

### Test 4 — Low Trust Required to Say Yes

The downside of saying yes is near zero. The offer structure has to be low-stakes enough that a stranger can agree to the next step. Pilots, proof-of-concepts, short paid tests, and free deliverables qualify. A 12-month retainer on first contact does not.

---

## The Language Tell

The fastest way to spot a warm offer is by the verbs.

**Cold offer verbs — generative, net-new:**  
Generate, build, get, land, book, source, add, bring in, produce, deliver, create, win, close

**Warm offer verbs — optimization and management:**  
Optimize, improve, manage, streamline, enhance, refine, scale, grow, support, partner, help with, handle, take over

💡 **Tip:** **Quick test:** Read your current positioning out loud. If the main verbs are from the second list, your offer needs a reframe.

---

## The Reframe Move: Warm Service → Cold Offer

The reframe is not to change what the service does. The reframe is to write the offer to the end business outcome — not to the service itself.

**Line 1:** End business outcome with a number and a timeframe. **Line 2:** The mechanism — "by doing X, Y, and Z." Line 1 is the offer. Line 2 is the service.

| Service Type | Warm Framing (the service) | Cold Framing (the outcome) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| MSP / Managed IT | "We manage your IT infrastructure and provide ongoing support" | "We'll cut your IT downtime by 40% and reduce support costs by $8K/month in the first 90 days" |
| Fractional CFO | "We provide fractional CFO services to growing businesses" | "We'll find $50K–$150K in recoverable margin in your first 60 days — or you don't pay" |
| AI Operations / Automation | "We automate your business processes using AI" | "We'll eliminate 20+ hours of manual ops work per week within 30 days" |
| RevOps Consulting | "We optimize your revenue operations and tech stack" | "We'll add 2–3 new qualified pipeline sources in 90 days without adding headcount" |
| Cost Recovery / Audit | "We audit your vendor contracts and expenses" | "We'll find $30K+ in recoverable costs in your first audit — guaranteed or free" |
| Cold Email Agency | "We manage your cold email outreach campaigns" | "We'll book 8–15 qualified meetings per month with your ideal clients in 90 days" |

---

## The Front-End / Back-End Structure (Wedge Offers)

Most clients instinctively try to sell their full engagement through cold email. This almost never works. The cold offer's job is to get the meeting — not to close the deal. The right structure is a **front-end wedge offer** that leads to the back-end real offer once trust is built on the call.

**Wedge offer examples:**

- A pilot or proof of concept
- A first 90-day engagement with a specific deliverable
- A paid audit or diagnostic
- A lead magnet or one-pager that leads to a call
- A short, finite engagement with a specific numeric outcome

💡 **Tip:** The back-end offer — the 12-month retainer, the full-service package, the ongoing relationship — is what gets sold on the call. It never goes in the email. If someone is trying to put their back-end offer into a cold email, the campaign will underperform regardless of how good the copy is.

---

## Risk Reversal — The 7 Levels

Risk reversal doesn't always have to appear in the email itself. What matters is that the offer structure is low-stakes enough that a stranger can say yes. From strongest to weakest:

1. **Pay on performance** — "You don't pay unless we hit X."
2. **Money-back guarantee** — "If we don't deliver X in Y days, full refund."
3. **Free pilot or free trial** — "Free 30-day pilot. If it works, we'll talk about next steps."
4. **Paid pilot with exit ramp** — "90-day engagement. No obligation to continue after."
5. **Free deliverable upfront** — "We'll build you a custom lead list just for getting on a call."
6. **"Even if you don't work with us"** — "I'll break down the strategy on a call even if you just want to take it and run with it."
7. **Case study match** — "We just did this for a similar [their exact business type]." (Social proof as implicit risk reversal.)

---

## The Anti-Patterns: "This Smells Like a Warm Offer"

**Vague partnership language:** "We'll be your growth partner" / "Let us be an extension of your team" → These assume the prospect already wants a partner. They don't.

**Optimization language:** "We'll optimize your X" / "We'll streamline your X" → These assume the prospect already believes their current X needs improving.

**Expertise-assumption language:** "Leverage our expertise in X" / "With 20 years of experience" → These require the prospect to already care about your credentials. Strangers don't.

**Open-ended CTAs:** "Book a strategy call to learn more" / "Let's chat about how we can help" → These offer nothing concrete. A good cold CTA promises something specific: a breakdown, a strategy, a free deliverable, a case study walkthrough.

**Process language:** "We handle everything from X to Y to Z" / "Full-service X" → Strangers don't want to hear about your process. They want to hear about their outcome.

---

## The Diagnostic Checklist

### The Four Tests

- Is there a specific number in the outcome?
- Is there a finite timeframe and scope?
- Does the offer produce new money or new opportunity — not optimize what already exists?
- Can a stranger say yes to the next step without needing to trust the sender?

### The Language

- Are the main verbs generative (generate, get, land, add, book) rather than optimization (optimize, manage, improve, streamline)?
- Is the offer written to the end business outcome — not to the service itself?

### The Structure

- Is there a clear wedge offer separate from the full back-end engagement?
- Is the wedge what's going in the email?

### The Safety

- Is the offer low-stakes enough that a stranger can say yes to the next step?
- If the ask is high-commitment, is there risk reversal bolted on?

⚠️ **Warning:** **Rule of thumb:** If the offer fails two or more tests, stop and reframe before writing any copy. No amount of good copywriting will save a warm offer in a cold channel.

sales/sales-mindset.title: Sales Mindset
sales/sales-mindset.content:
# Sales Mindset

## Chain of Beliefs Explanation

Imagine someone says, *"I can jump over a moving car."* Your first reaction would likely be skepticism. But what would need to be true for you to believe them?

Now suppose they add: *"I'm 6'10" and have been playing basketball for 15+ years. I earned a full-ride scholarship to a Division 1 college. You can check my highlights on LSU's website, including a video of me dunking over a 6'6" center."*

At this point, their claim feels believable — they've provided credibility, social proof, and reasoning that supports their ability.

---

## What's the Point?

💡 **Tip:** In order for someone to believe something they need to be given proof.

This same framework applies to creating offers. Let's say someone approaches you and says, *"I can double your organic traffic in 90 days."* Your immediate reaction might be skepticism: *"Who are you? Have you actually done this before? Why should I believe you?"*

Now imagine their pitch goes like this: *"I'm Joe, an SEO consultant with experience working for multiple 8- and 9-figure companies. In my last 7 projects, I've doubled inbound traffic within 90 days. Would that be helpful to you?"* Far more convincing — you now have context, proof, and credibility.

💡 **Tip:** **If you say a statement, think about what NEEDS to be true in order for that statement to be believable & make someone trust you.**

sales/sales-positioning.title: Positioning
sales/sales-positioning.content:
# Positioning

Positioning is the cornerstone of sales and business success. It's about finding your unique spot in the minds of your target audience and showing why you're the best choice for their needs.

Think of positioning as creating a strong mental association between your agency and the results your clients want. On sales calls, positioning means guiding prospects through objections and framing your offer as the exact solution they need — before objections even arise.

**Good positioning explains:**

- What sets you apart.
- Why you're the solution they've been looking for.
- How your services align with their goals.

---

## Why Is Positioning Important?

Positioning shapes how people perceive your agency and whether they decide to work with you. It influences your ability to close sales, retain clients, and charge higher prices.

### Key Benefits of Strong Positioning

1. **Standing Out:** Cut through the noise and make it clear why you're different and better.
2. **Clarity for Clients:** Help them easily understand why they should choose you.
3. **Marketing Direction:** A strong position drives consistent, effective messaging across all your marketing efforts.
4. **Attracting Ideal Clients:** Speak the language of the clients you want, drawing in the ones who align best with your expertise.
5. **Commanding Higher Prices:** With clear positioning, clients see your value and are willing to pay more for it.

---

## Pricing Is About Perceived Value

Positioning directly affects what you can charge. The difference between agencies charging $150 per call, $3.5K monthly retainer, $30K one-time system setup and $150K per year consulting with revenue share is positioning.

Lead generation is the core of all these offers — it's the way they're positioned that allows people selling them to charge higher prices. Similar value, completely different perception.

### How to Elevate Your Positioning

1. **Change Your Pricing Model:** Move from pay-per-call to retainers or performance-based pricing.
2. **Provide Added Value:** Offer extras like sales scripts, call frameworks, LinkedIn optimization, or pipeline management.
3. **Niche Down:** Focus on serving a specific industry where you can build deep expertise and deliver outsized value.

### Examples of Improved Positioning

- A **copywriter** moves from charging $1,000 for web copy to earning $10,000+ per client by taking a percentage of conversion uplifts.
- An **SEO agency** shifts from "getting rankings" to focusing on directly increasing client revenue, enabling premium pricing.
- A **lead gen agency** evolves from selling a fixed number of leads to becoming an **outsourced sales department** responsible for measurable revenue growth.

---

## Positioning in Sales

To position your offer effectively during sales calls, you **need** to understand your prospect's pain points and goals. Double the time spent in the discovery phase of the sales call to deeply understand what your client is looking for.

### Tailoring Your Positioning

**Scenario:** The prospect tried lead generation before but got poor results because sales calls were unqualified, wasting the CEO's time.

**How to Position Your Offer:**

- Highlight that your service delivers only **triple-verified leads** to ensure high-intent sales calls.
- Emphasize how this protects the CEO's time while increasing their close rate.
- Include additional value like sales process setup and lead nurturing strategies.

Now, your offer is not just lead generation — it's a comprehensive solution to their specific struggles.

---

## How to Create a Winning Positioning Strategy

1. **Understand Your Target Market:** Know their challenges, desires, and what keeps them up at night.
2. **Analyze Competitors:** Identify gaps in the market and position yourself to fill them.
3. **Clarify Your Unique Value Proposition (UVP):** Clearly communicate the outcomes only you can deliver.
4. **Communicate Consistently:** Ensure all messaging aligns with your positioning.
5. **Leverage Social Proof:** Use testimonials and case studies to validate your claims.
6. **Adapt and Evolve:** Revisit your positioning regularly to ensure it stays relevant and effective.

💡 **Tip:** Stacking your value is a SUPER EASY way for you to better position your offer and its value, allowing you in some cases, quite literally, to charge 10x more for the SAME thing!

sales/lead-magnets.title: Lead Magnets
sales/lead-magnets.content:
# Lead Magnets

Lead magnets are a powerful way to increase outbound response rates by offering potential customers a no-brainer value proposition. The key is to offer real value and tailor them to your audience.

---

## Why Lead Magnets Work

- They address a specific problem or need for your prospect.
- They decrease the barrier to reply by providing immediate value upfront, making prospects more likely to engage even if they are not ready to commit to a call or purchase.
- Instead of persuading prospects, show them evidence of your success. This will make their decision to take the next step easier.

---

## Types of Lead Magnets

### 1. Typical Lead Magnets

**Definition:** Provide resources (e.g., documents, reports, insights, videos, or templates) for prospects to use independently.

**Examples:** An eBook explaining how to optimize email marketing; a video tutorial on improving sales processes. While effective, these often lack the personal touch needed to get prospects on a call.

### 2. Service-Based Lead Magnets

**Definition:** Offer a service that requires effort, directly solving a problem the prospect faces.

**Examples:**

- "I'll script and edit 1 free YouTube video for you. If you like it, we can discuss creating more."
- "Free ad creative for them to test out — no strings attached."
- "I will give you 500 leads from your ICP without any charge or commitment."

---

## Strategies to Increase Responses with Lead Magnets

### 1. Offer Real Value

Your lead magnet should solve a specific problem for your prospect. Instead of general content, offer actionable insights or results: *"Here's the exact strategy and email templates we used to book 451 demos for a supply chain management software client in three months."*

### 2. Tailor to Your Audience

Understand your audience's challenges and create lead magnets that directly address their needs. Research their pain points and personalize the offering to show relevance.

### 3. Highlight Your Expertise

All businesses operate on knowledge arbitrage. Use your lead magnets to demonstrate your knowledge and experience. Share detailed strategies, proven templates, and relevant case studies.

### 4. Social Proof

Build trust by showcasing how your strategies have worked for others in the same industry. Include testimonials, metrics and results, and stories of past successes.

### 5. Clear Call to Action (CTA)

Ensure that your lead magnet includes a clear next step: "Book a call to learn more" or "Let's schedule a session to review these insights for your business." Guide the prospect seamlessly from engaging with the lead magnet to scheduling a conversation.

sales/sales-assets.title: Sales Assets
sales/sales-assets.content:
# Sales Assets

💡 **Tip:** **Placeholder Video — How to Use Claude to Create Pitch Decks, Sales Assets, Marketing Material & ROI Calculators with HTML**

---

Sales and marketing assets are important tools and materials that help salespeople sell better.

**Examples:**

- **Pitch Decks:** Quick presentations to introduce your company and what you offer.
- **Case Studies:** Stories about how your company helped customers, showing your success.
- **Sales Decks:** Detailed slides used in sales meetings to explain benefits and handle concerns.
- **One-Pagers / White Papers:** Detailed documents explaining industry trends and your offerings.
- **ROI Calculators:** Tools to help prospects see the financial benefits of your product or service.
- **Educational Content:** Blog posts, webinars, or reports that share useful information with your audience.

---

## Why They're Important

- **Educate and Engage:** Good presentations and content can catch the interest of potential clients by showing your value.
- **Show Success:** Case studies prove your solutions work.
- **Facilitate Sales:** These tools help keep sales conversations focused and useful.
- **Demonstrate Value:** Calculators help prospects see how your product can benefit them financially.
- **Build Credibility:** Well-researched content makes your company look knowledgeable and trustworthy.

---

## Using Assets in Sales

### 1. Outreach Stage

Use presentations/content to introduce your company and build credibility. Example: asking if you can send your ROI calculator.

### 2. Pre-Call Stage

Share case studies, reports, and white papers to explain how your product solves problems and build credibility.

### 3. Decision Stage

Use tailored presentations and calculators to address concerns and show value.

---

## The Easiest Way To Share Your Sales Material With Prospects

The easiest way to do this is through Calendly (or your call booking tool).

1. Upload your files to Google Drive
2. Make sure the files are open and accessible to anyone with the link
3. Go to [Google Link Generator](https://sites.google.com/site/gdocs2direct/) and follow the instructions to get your downloadable link
4. Amend your notification email that is sent to prospects when they book a call
5. Include the links for prospects to download the guides

sales/qualifying-prospects.title: Qualifying Prospects
sales/qualifying-prospects.content:
# Qualifying Prospects

## Ideal Client Profile (ICP)

### 1. What Is an Ideal Client Profile (ICP)?

An Ideal Client Profile (ICP) is a detailed description of the perfect customer for your business — a blueprint for identifying companies most likely to benefit from and value your services.

Your ICP includes key details such as:

- **Type of Business:** What industry are they in?
- **Location:** Where are they based?
- **Size:** How big is their team?
- **Revenue:** What's their annual income?
- **Decision-Makers:** Who makes the purchasing decisions?
- **Pain Points:** What challenges can your service solve for them?
- **Spending Habits:** How much do they typically invest in services like yours?

### 2. Why Is an ICP Important?

- **Saves Time and Money:** Focus on the right prospects instead of wasting resources on leads that don't fit.
- **Improves Service Quality:** Tailor your offerings to perfectly meet the needs of your ideal clients.
- **Increases Customer Satisfaction:** ICP-aligned clients are more likely to love your service and stay loyal.
- **Boosts Revenue:** Targeted outreach leads to more conversions, higher close rates, and bigger deals.

### 3. What to Consider When Building Your ICP

**Demographics and Basics:** Industry, Location, Size (headcount), Revenue range.

**Behavior and Needs:** Decision-Makers (e.g., CEO, CMO, VP of Sales); Pain Points; Spending Habits (average deal size, ideally $3K or more).

**Market Insights:** Close Rate; Market Size (is there enough demand to scale?); Case Studies (do you have 2+ success stories in this niche?).

### 4. Market Research on Your ICP

Market research is the SINGLE biggest difference that separates average campaigns from excellent campaigns. Time spent better understanding your market is never wasted. Once you truly understand their pain points and how you solve them, your emails write themselves.

### 5. Be as Specific as You Can

If more than 20% of the prospects you get on a call with are 100% qualified, there's something wrong with your ICP. Make it more specific and only agree to work with people who you're CERTAIN you'll be able to get results for.

### 6. An Example ICP

- Niche — B2B SaaS companies offering Enterprise Software
- Average Annual Deal size — $10,000+
- Close rate — 20%+ on warmed appointments
- Minimum 2 outcome-focused case studies in the specific target market
- Total Addressable Market — 10K+
- Revenue of $3M per year / 10–150 employees
- Companies in the US, UK, Canada
- C-level & senior executives (brand director, head of marketing, VP of Sales, etc.)

### 7. Prepare ROI Breakdowns for Different Scenarios

| Scenario | Close Rate | Show-Up Rate | Outcome | ROI |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Worst Case | 20% | 70% | 8 booked → 4 meetings → 1 client ($10K) | 4X |
| Average | 30% | 80% | 5 booked → 4 meetings → 1 client ($10K) | 5.25X |
| Best Case | 40% | 90% | 4 booked → 3 meetings → 1 client ($10K) | 7.3X |

⚠️ **Warning:** Anything below the worst-case level means you can't provide ROI — don't take them on. For prospects like this, consider a downsell: sell them a guide to set up your service in-house. Don't leave money on the table.

### 8. Refine Your ICP

Refining your ICP is an ongoing process. Regularly analyze your best clients to identify shared traits, challenges, and characteristics. Use this data to tailor your **offer**, **value proposition**, and **positioning** for similar prospects.

---

## Qualifying Prospects

Qualifying is essential. Without it, you risk wasting time, energy, and resources on leads that aren't suited to your services. When you're booking meetings through cold email, prospects won't fill out a form — qualification has to happen in one of two ways:

### Option 1 — Qualify via Calendly Form (Inbound / Organic Leads)

For any inbound traffic, VSL viewers, or organic leads clicking a booking link, add qualification questions directly to your Calendly booking form.

**Questions to add:**

- What does your business do and who do you sell to?
- What's your current monthly revenue?
- How many clients/customers are you looking to add per month?
- Have you tried cold outreach or lead generation before? What happened?
- What's your budget for this kind of service?
- Is there anything specific you're looking to solve right now?

### Option 2 — Qualify on the Call (First 5 Minutes)

For cold email bookings, qualification happens in the first 5 minutes of the call. Don't pitch until you've done this — it's the most important part of the call.

**Opening questions:**

- *"What made you want to jump on a call today?"*
- *"What's going on in the business right now that makes this feel relevant?"*
- *"Is this something you're looking to solve soon, or are you more at the exploring stage right now?"*
- *"Have you tried anything like this before? What happened?"*
- *"Just so I can tailor this conversation — what does your current pipeline look like?"*

💡 **Tip:** **Rule of thumb:** If they're not ready, not the right fit, or don't have the budget — disqualify fast and move on. Your time is your most valuable asset.

sales/sales-process.title: Sales Process
sales/sales-process.content:
# Sales Process

Now that you understand how to qualify prospects before a call, it's time to implement a proven sales process to ensure a high show-up rate and handle prospect's objections before they come to the call.

[View Two-Call Close Sales Process Board on Miro →](https://miro.com/app/board/uXjVLufOkhk=/)

💡 **Tip:** Make a copy and use for yourself by going to "Board" → "Make A Copy". Set this up for your clients to increase their ROI and decrease the likelihood that they'll churn.

---

## Maximising Call Show-Up Rates

**Target: Aim for an 80% call show-up rate.**

### 1. Scheduling

Scheduling calls within 48–72 hours can increase show-up rates by up to 30%. Don't book too far in advance — calls scheduled more than 12 days out have lower show-up rates. Ideally no more than 7 days out.

### 2. Minimise Time Slots

Rather than offering wide open availability, limit your time slots to convey a sense of scarcity and value. Limiting availability has been shown to improve show-up rates by around 5%.

### 3. Optimise Call Scheduling Time

Avoid early morning or late evening slots. The **10am–2pm window** sees a 25% higher show-up rate compared to calls scheduled outside this timeframe.

### 4. Calendar Reminders

Sending a reminder SMS 1-hour before the call can reduce no-shows by up to 20%.

**Recommended Calendly Reminder Process:**

- 24-hours before
- 4-hours before
- 1-hour before

### 5. Provide Pre-Call Value

Providing pre-call value has been shown to increase show-up rates by 15% and close rate by 8%. Share decks/one-pagers through Calendly notification emails that include: intro your company + team, FAQs, what to expect on the call, your process, how to prep, case studies, and a thank you page.

### 6. Confirm Interest and Availability

MAKE SURE prospects are actually interested and ask them to reconfirm their availability in your reminders. Re-confirmation of the meeting can increase show-up rates by 10% compared to purely automated messages.

### 7. Use Calendly Workflow To Reschedule Cancelled Meetings

Create a Workflow inside Calendly that auto-sends a reschedule link on cancellation. This maximises your chances of getting people to rebook without having to manually follow up.

sales/call-show-up.title: Call Show-Up Process
sales/call-show-up.content:
# Call Show-Up Process

⚠️ **Warning:** **QUICK NOTE:** This simple pre-call process will get you 80%+ show up rate consistently.

## What This Page Will Help You Do

- Set up automated Calendly reminders that actually work
- Edit the default templates to sound like you (not a robot)
- Handle cancellations automatically without chasing
- Never wonder "will they show up?" again

⚠️ **Warning:** You should be manually booking your calls, not using links. This process assumes you're inputting call info into Calendly yourself, after manually confirming times with prospects.

---

## The Proven Call Show-Up Process

| When | Channel | What It Sends | Why It Matters |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Immediately | Email | Confirmation + Zoom + proof + agenda | Sets tone, proves trust |
| 24hr Before | Email | Reminder + re-confirmation | Reduces no-shows by 10% |
| 1hr Before | Email | Reminder + outside value | Rebuilds desire to attend |
| On cancel | Email | "Reschedule your call" + link | Saves pipeline without chasing |

---

## 1. Set Up Your Calendly Event Properly

Open Calendly → Events → Find your sales call → Click "Edit"

Under **"When can people book this event?"**

- **Duration:** 30 minutes (or whatever your call length is)
- **Availability:** Consider offering 10am–4pm slots (highest show-up window)
- **Date range:** Don't let them book more than 7 days out

*Why: Mid-day calls have 25% higher show-up rates and prospects who want to speak more than 7 days out aren't as interested as we want them to be.*

---

## 2. Edit Your Reminder Emails (Don't skip this)

Navigate to: **Event Settings → Notifications & Workflows → Email Reminders**

Don't use Calendly's default templates as-is. Click "Edit" on each and customise:

### Immediate Confirmation Email

```
Hey {invitee_first_name}, You're all set for {event_date} at {event_time}.

Here's our [Pre-Call Guide] — this will [explain what it will go over and the
outcome as to why they should read it before the call].

Looking forward to speaking with you.
Talk soon, [Your Name]
```

### 24-Hour Before Email

```
Hi {invitee_first_name}, Looking forward to speaking with you tomorrow at {event_time}.

Reminder, here's our [Pre-call guide + why they should review before call].

If something's come up, just hit reply and we'll find a better time.
[Your Name]
```

### 1-Hour Before Email

```
Hi {invitee_first_name}, In 1 hour we'll walk through:

[Bullet points of how you will help/what you will share]

Zoom link: {location}
See you at {event_time}, [Your Name]
```

**Calendly Placeholders You Can Use:** {invitee\_first\_name}, {invitee\_full\_name}, {event\_name}, {event\_date}, {event\_time}, {location}

---

## 3. Handle Cancellations Automatically

This is where most founders lose deals. Someone cancels, you forget to follow up — deal lost.

**Fix:** Create a Calendly Workflow that auto-sends a reschedule link.

1. Go to Workflows → Create Workflow
2. Trigger: "Event is cancelled"
3. Wait: 5 minutes after cancellation
4. Action: Send email to invitee

### Auto-Reschedule Email Template

*Subject: Want to pick a better time?*

```
Hi {invitee_first_name}, saw that you are now unable to make our call.

Here's our link to reschedule and find a more suitable time — {reschedule_url}

Looking forward to speaking with you soon.
[Your Name]
```

sales/pre-sell-guide.title: Pre-Sell With A Pre-Call Guide
sales/pre-sell-guide.content:
# Pre-Sell With A Pre-Call Guide

⚠️ **Warning:** **QUICK NOTE:** Most founders book a call and then go silent until it happens. A simple pre-call guide can increase your close rate by 15–25% because prospects show up half-sold.

## What This Page Will Help You Do

- Understand what a pre-call guide actually is (and why it works)
- Know exactly what to include to build trust and set expectations
- Have a proven template you can customise and use immediately
- Know when and how to share it for maximum impact

---

## What a Pre-Call Guide Is

A pre-call guide is a simple document (usually 5–10 pages) that you send to prospects after they book a call with you.

It's **NOT** a pitch deck. It **IS** a trust-building asset that:

- Sets clear expectations for the call
- Shows social proof and credibility
- Gets them thinking about their situation beforehand
- Eliminates common objections before you even talk
- Makes them more committed to showing up

**Think of it as doing half your sales work before the call starts.**

---

## Why You Should Use One

- Does most of the heavy lifting before the call starts
- Lets you reference sections during the call instead of explaining from scratch
- Gets them thinking about their situation beforehand
- Eliminates basic questions and objections upfront
- Makes prospects more committed (they've invested time learning about you)

Most of your prospects have never heard of you before booking the call. Without a pre-call guide, you spend the first 15 minutes of every call building credibility and explaining what you do.

---

## What to Include in Your Pre-Call Guide

1. **What to Expect:** Brief overview of what the call will cover. Sets the agenda and reduces anxiety.
2. **Why This Call Is Worth Your Time:** 4–6 bullet points explaining the value they'll get. Focus on insights, not your services.
3. **About Us / Meet The Team:** Quick credibility builder. How long you've been doing this, what you're proud of, team photo.
4. **Who You'll Be Speaking With:** Personal introduction from you. Put a face to the name. Include a LinkedIn connection CTA.
5. **Frequently Asked Questions:** Address the 4–5 questions you get on every call. Price ranges, timeline, what's included, etc.
6. **Success Stories (3–4 Case Studies):** Real results for similar businesses. Include company type, specific outcomes, and what made it work.
7. **ROI Breakdown:** Simple visual showing how working with you pays for itself. Keep it conservative.
8. **Preparing For Our Call:** Tell them what to bring: current metrics, goals, budget range. Makes the call more productive.

---

## When and How to Share It

**Timing:** Include the link in your Immediate Confirmation email; mention it again in your 24-hour reminder.

**Where to Host It:** We recommend **Gamma** ([gamma.app](https://gamma.app)) — super easy to use and can be edited with AI. You can also use Notion, Google Slides, Canva, or a simple PDF.

---

## The Template

We've created a complete pre-call guide template you can copy and customise. All 8 sections with placeholder text, professional design that works on any device, ready to customise with your info.

💡 **Tip:** Download the **Pre-Call Guide Template** (PDF) from the original Notion page. Setup Time: 30 minutes to customise with your details.

This isn't about being fancy. It's about doing the work upfront so your sales calls are easier and more profitable.

sales/set-the-frame.title: Set The Frame + Own The Call
sales/set-the-frame.content:
# Set The Frame + Own The Call

⚠️ **Warning:** **QUICK NOTE:** Most people let prospects drive the call. Big mistake. If you don't set the agenda, they will. And their agenda is usually "tell me everything so I can think about it later." Your agenda should be "diagnose their situation and lead them to a decision."

## What This Page Will Help You Do

- Set a clear agenda that keeps you in control of the call flow
- Build rapport without wasting time on small talk
- Lead the conversation through a proven structure
- Handle prospects who try to derail or rush the process

---

## Why Most Founders Lose Control

**They skip the agenda setting.** Without a clear structure, calls become chaotic. Prospects ask random questions, jump to pricing too early, or try to get you to "send something over" without qualifying themselves.

The result? You spend 30 minutes answering questions about your service instead of diagnosing their problems. Then they say "sounds interesting, can you send me some information?"

---

## How to Set The Agenda (The 30-Second Frame That Controls Everything)

> "Great. So [Name], as you're looking for [your service] what I'd like to do is share some information about us and why we reached out. I'll ask a few questions to understand your business and what you're looking for. IF WE CAN HELP AND YOU'RE A FIT, I'll walk you through exactly what we do and I'll share details on the offer and we'll discuss next steps. Sound good?"

**Why this works:** You've told them exactly what's happening. They feel in control (because you asked "sound good?"). But YOU are actually in control of the flow and can refer back to this if they try to jump ahead.

---

## How To Handle Common Disruptions

### "What do you guys do exactly?"

> "Great question — that's actually what I want to walk you through. Let me just ask a couple questions first to understand your situation, then I can show you exactly how we'd help someone in your position."

### "Can you just send me some information?"

> "Of course I can send something over. Just so I send the right information, help me understand [your situation/goals]. That way I can customise what I send."

### "What does this cost?"

> "Pricing depends on your specific situation. Let me understand what you're looking for first, then I can give you exact numbers that make sense for your business."

### "I need to think about it"

> "Of course — this is an important decision. What specifically do you need to think through? Maybe I can help clarify those points now."

---

## Common Mistakes That Kill Your Frame

- ❌ **Answering every question immediately** — They ask about pricing, you give pricing without context
- ❌ **Letting them control the flow** — "Actually, before we get into that, can you tell me about your guarantee?"
- ❌ **Not setting expectations** — You just start asking questions without explaining why
- ❌ **Rushing through discovery** — You want to get to your pitch instead of finding their pain

---

## Your Frame Control Checklist

- I will set the agenda in the first couple of minutes
- I will not answer pricing questions until I understand their situation
- I will not move to the solution until I find something to anchor against
- I will get clear next steps before ending the call
- I control this conversation while making them feel heard

---

## The Mindset Shift

| Old Thinking | New Thinking |
| --- | --- |
| "I need to impress them with how much I know" | "I need to diagnose their situation" |
| "I should answer all their questions" | "I should ask better questions than they've ever been asked" |
| "I hope they like my service" | "I need to understand if we're a good fit" |

sales/call-prep.title: Call Prep — Set Yourself Up to Win
sales/call-prep.content:
# Call Prep — Set Yourself Up to Win

⚠️ **Warning:** **QUICK NOTE:** The difference between closing 10% and 40%+ of calls often comes down to the 15 minutes before you start the call.

## What This Page Will Help You Do

- Know exactly how to research prospects in under 10 minutes
- Have all the materials and numbers ready to close confidently
- Feel mentally prepared and in control before every call
- Never wing another sales call again

---

## The 4-Stage Pre-Call Prep System

### 1. Research Their Business (5–7 minutes)

**What You're Looking For:** Their likely target market and ICP; services they offer; case studies or results on their website; company size and maturity; any recent news, funding, or growth signals.

- Visit their website — note their main services
- Check their "About" or "Case Studies" page for client types
- Look at their LinkedIn for company size and recent posts
- Note any specific industries they mention targeting
- Identify if they're B2B or B2C focused

💡 **Tip:** **Tip:** Don't over-research. 7 minutes max. You'll learn more on the call through proper discovery.

### 2. Know Your Numbers Cold

- Your guarantee/core offer details
- 2–3 relevant case studies with specific numbers
- Your value stack breakdown with market values
- Typical close rates for their industry (for ROI calculations)
- Your pricing structure and payment terms

### 3. Technical Preparation

- Agreement/contract prepared and ready to send
- Payment links or invoice templates ready
- Calendar for immediate follow-up scheduling
- Value stack document or breakdown accessible
- Any relevant case study documents or testimonials

**Quick Access Setup:** Bookmark your calendar scheduling link; have agreement template saved with their name pre-filled; keep case studies in a folder for quick access; save your value stack as a quick-reference document.

### 4. Mental Preparation & Confidence

- This is a conversation, not a presentation — stay flexible but follow the structure
- You're the expert — they need help
- Never skip discovery — No anchor = no leverage
- Always set next steps — control the follow-up process
- Use their numbers — make it about them, not you
- Be prepared to walk away — desperation kills deals

> "I'm here to help them solve a real problem. If I can't help them, I'll tell them honestly and refer them to someone who can. My job is to understand their situation and determine if we're a fit."

---

## Final Pre-Call Checklist (2 Minutes Before The Call)

- Deep breath — you're prepared
- Review their company name and your research notes
- Have your case studies accessible
- Clear your desktop of distractions
- Remember: you're here to help, not to convince

---

## Common Preparation Mistakes to Avoid

- ❌ **Over-researching** — Don't spend 30 minutes researching. You'll learn more through discovery
- ❌ **Under-preparing** — Don't wing it completely. 15 minutes of prep beats hoping for the best
- ❌ **Forgetting case studies** — Have 2–3 relevant examples ready with specific numbers
- ❌ **No technical setup** — Scrambling for agreements mid-call kills momentum
- ❌ **Wrong mindset** — You're not begging for business, you're evaluating fit

**Remember:** Preparation breeds confidence. Confidence closes deals.

sales/sales-call-strategies.title: Sales Call Strategies
sales/sales-call-strategies.content:
# Sales Call Strategies

💡 **Tip:** We recommend using either a one OR two call process when closing prospects.

## Which Call Process Should You Choose?

Picking the right way to approach sales calls is super important. Some people like to close leads with just one call, while others prefer to split it into two. Each method has its own pros depending on what you're selling and who you're selling to.

---

## The One Call Close

The one call close process is straightforward — you try to close the deal in just one call. It works well for traditional lead generation such as pay per call.

### When to Use

- **Low to Medium Complexity Offers:** Ideal for simple products or services — think PPC, lead gen
- **Highly Qualified Leads:** Best when the prospect has a clear and pressing need

### Advantages

- **Efficiency:** Saves time for both you and the prospect, allowing for fewer steps to close a deal
- **Momentum:** Capitalises on the prospect's initial interest, personally accelerating chances to close

---

## Two Call Close

With the two call close, you split the sales process into two parts: (1) **Discovery** — you gather info and build a connection, (2) **Solution** — you present your solution and try to seal the deal.

### When to Use

- **More complex offers:** Things like retainer deals with set-up, selling systems or growth consulting
- **Educational Needs:** If the prospect needs more information or education about the service

### Advantages

- **Less Pressure:** Some people feel less pressured during a two call process
- **Enhanced Qualification:** Provides an opportunity to better qualify leads
- **Relationship Building:** Helps build stronger connections with prospects, crucial for long-term business relationships

---

## Choosing the Right Approach

- Selling traditional, low-friction lead gen such as pay-per-meeting → **one call close**
- Selling a more complex offer such as systems, growth consulting → **two call close**
- New to sales and not fully confident yet → **start with the two call close**

---

## The One-Call Framework

1. **Introduction & Rapport Building:** Begin with a friendly introduction and some small talk to create rapport.
2. **Company Presentation:** Briefly introduce your company, share your achievements, background, and success stories. Address any potential objections before they ask.
3. **Prospect Qualification & Deep Dive:** Ask targeted questions to determine their fit and understand their situation — business, ICP, sales process, CPAs, etc.
4. **Process Explanation:** Outline how you typically work with clients and what they can expect. Make clear what is expected of them in terms of time and commitment.
5. **Present Your Solution + ROI Breakdown:** Explain what you will do for them based on their needs. Breakdown the VALUE you will create and the ROI they can expect.
6. **The Close:** Tell them/ask if they wish to close. If they have any objections, address those and bring them back to this place.

[📹 Watch This Webinar To Learn How To One-Call Close →](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0oxYEzz-fhY)

---

## The Two-Call Framework

### Call 1 — Discovery

1. **Introduction & Rapport Building:** Begin with a friendly introduction and some small talk to create rapport.
2. **Company Presentation:** Briefly introduce your company, share your achievements and success stories. Address any potential objections they may have.
3. **Prospect Qualification & Deep Dive:** Ask targeted questions to determine their fit and understand their needs.
4. **Process Explanation:** Outline how you typically work with clients. Make it clear that a detailed solution will be presented during the second call.
5. **Setting Expectations and Scheduling 2nd Call:** Go into the time and date of the second call. Inform them that they will receive marketing materials prior to the next meeting.

### Pre-2nd Call

Before the second call, send them a **process deck** (outline your process, timeline, value FAQs, ROI calculator specific to their ICP) and a **case study deck** of your relevant case studies.

💡 **Tip:** Doing this puts us in a stronger position on the second call — we've built credibility and addressed potential objections prior to speaking again.

### Call 2 — The Solution

1. **Recap of Discovery Call:** Start with a short recap of the key points discussed during the discovery call.
2. **Solution Presentation:** Present the customised solution. Show them: how many leads you can contact, quality of those leads, current campaign results, how the set up works, monthly price.
3. **Value Stack and ROI Breakdown:** Walk them through the ROI by providing them with the potential return on investment. This solidifies their confidence in the sale and justifies your cost.
4. **Price Presentation:** Break down the cost of your solution. Ensure you relate this back to the value they will receive and the ROI you previously discussed.
5. **Closing:** Address any final questions or objections, and if they are ready, move towards close. Be soft.

sales/value-stacking.title: Value Stacking
sales/value-stacking.content:
# Value Stacking

## 1. What is Value Stacking?

Value stacking is a powerful sales strategy that involves highlighting and combining the individual components of your service to emphasize their collective value. Instead of presenting your offer as a single solution, you break it down to show how each piece contributes to the overall outcome, making the value more tangible and compelling to your client.

---

## 2. Why Use Value Stacking?

- **Maximises Perceived Value:** By demonstrating how each component contributes to the desired outcome, you effectively amplify the perceived value of your offer.
- **Differentiates Your Service:** Value stacking allows you to stand out in a crowded market by offering a multi-dimensional solution that addresses various aspects of a client's need.
- **Facilitates Up-selling and Cross-selling:** Introducing additional services becomes more natural and logical, encouraging clients to invest more.
- **Increases Revenue:** By showcasing a higher overall value, you can justify a higher price point.

---

## 3. The Power of Value Stacking

You sell traditional pay-per-meeting lead generation and typically price it at $300 per call. You want to charge more but struggle justifying it. Let's say you want to shift to a monthly retainer with a set-up fee.

**Instead of telling them you'll get them qualified prospects for $300 a call, go through this value stack on the call:**

| Service | Description | Market Value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Email Deliverability Optimisation | Monitoring and optimisation of your emails for optimal delivery + proven steps to manage email success across your domains | $500/month |
| Monthly Lead Generation | Monthly comprehensive lead generation services | $3,000/month |
| Lead Nurturing | Strategic lead nurturing campaigns, drastically increasing the number of prospects ready to buy within 3–12 months | $1,500/month |
| Inbox Management | Dedicated inbox management by our in-house team, focusing on securing meetings for your business | $1,500/month |
| Training Guides and SOPs | Exhaustive guides and Standard Operating Procedures designed for management and staff to enhance efficiency | $1,000/month |
| Marketing Content Creation | Compelling marketing content tailored to your audience, including persuasive sales letters and interactive web pages | $500/month |
| Unlimited Verified & Qualified Lead Lists | A never-ending supply of high-quality leads for your sales team, expanding beyond your target locations | $800/month |
| **Total** | **All Services** | **Market Value: $8,800/month** |

Can you see how by simply breaking down the THINGS YOU WOULD HAVE ALREADY DONE FOR A CLIENT and attributing a market value to them, it highlights just how valuable what you're offering is?

**You can then say:** *"So John, the total market value of our service is $8,800 but we will do that for you for $4,250 per month."*

It's hard for John to argue against that price when you present it like this and use ACTUAL market value pricing. Try it.

sales/upselling.title: Upselling
sales/upselling.content:
# Upselling

Upselling is something everyone should look to do and is a crucial strategy in any sales process, but particularly in lead generation.

---

## Why Upsell?

- Increase client CLTV
- Provide clients with more value to build authority in your space
- Earn more money

---

## Upsell Example

A simple up-sell in lead generation is lead nurturing. Here's how you can present it:

Only 3% of a target market is ready to buy at any given time. 41% of a target market will be ready to buy in 3–12 months.

By offering a lead nurturing program as an upsell, you can help your clients stay top of mind for the majority who will be ready to buy in the future.

**Example:** You could offer lead nurturing as an add-on service at $1,500 per month. You would explain to clients that while only 3% of the market is immediately ready to buy, 41% will be ready in 3–12 months. Nurturing leads over time significantly increases the chances of conversion, providing immense long-term value that compounds the number of leads you generate.

---

## Other Potential Upsell Options

- Company wide email deliverability improvement and monitoring
- Sales processes + increased conversions
- Sales training
- Offer review, market research and competitor analysis
