onboarding/onboarding-calls.title: Onboarding Calls
onboarding/onboarding-calls.content:
# Onboarding Calls

The onboarding call is the last piece of the client onboarding process — and one of the most important things you'll do in the entire client relationship. It might not apply to every situation, but if it makes sense for your service, implement it every single time.

---

## When to Do It

The onboarding call happens **after** all the foundational steps are complete:

1. Service agreement signed
2. Payment collected
3. Client added to your professional workspaces (Slack, Trello, etc.)
4. Onboarding form submitted and reviewed

Only once the client has done those things should you schedule the onboarding call. This gives you time to review their information, identify any gaps, and come prepared with specific questions and materials.

**Format:** Zoom, camera on. Aim for 30–45 minutes.

---

## Why the Onboarding Call Matters

### 1. It Builds Credibility and Trust From Day One

For most clients, this is the first time they're meeting the people actually doing the work. Getting on Zoom with your camera on gives them immediate reassurance that they're in good hands. This call establishes the relationship — and the trust you build here has a direct effect on how long clients stay with you.

### 2. It Clears Confusion Before It Becomes a Problem

You're likely about to onboard them into multiple platforms — a Slack workspace, a Trello board, an onboarding form, a payment system. There are always questions. The onboarding call gives them a dedicated space to ask those questions and get answers in real time.

### 3. It Lets You Fill In the Gaps

Clients don't always fill out the onboarding form the way you'd hope. The onboarding call is your workshop session — a chance to review what they gave you, ask follow-up questions, and make sure you have everything you need before work begins. This is also when you handle account access (LinkedIn logins, 2FA, etc.) while they're live on the call.

### 4. It Sets Expectations Early

The only time clients churn is when expectations don't match reality. The only time that happens is when expectations weren't properly set to begin with. The onboarding call is your opportunity to hear what your client is expecting, make sure it aligns with what you can actually deliver, and correct anything that's off — before it becomes a problem months later.

---

## The Onboarding Call Agenda

### Phase 1: Introductions

Start by introducing yourself, your team, and each person's specific role in the project. Be clear and specific — tell them exactly what you're responsible for so they know who to come to for what.

**Example structure:**

- *"I handle the overall strategy and targeting — so if you have questions about the lead list or the market we're going after, come to me."*
- *"[Team member] writes all the scripts and messaging — any feedback on copy, that's their lane."*
- *"[Team member] is your point of contact for sales questions — once leads start coming in, they can help you make the most of them."*

### Phase 2: Hear From the Client

Once introductions are done, turn it over to the client. Ask them to give you a quick overview of:

- Who they are and their role
- Where the business is right now
- Who else on their team might be involved in this project
- **Most importantly: What are their goals? What does success look like for them?**

This is critical. You touched on this in the onboarding form, but you need to hear it directly — and you need to make sure what they're expecting is actually achievable. If their expectations are unrealistic, set them straight here. Not harshly — but clearly. Expectation management from day one is the foundation of long-term retention.

### Phase 3: The Screen Share

Share your screen and walk them through your workspace. Show them where everything lives — their login credentials, their Trello board, their assets. Then walk them through the specific work you've done so far. For a lead generation service, this is typically:

- **The leads list** — show them where to find it and ask them to review and approve it
- **The scripts** — read through what you've written and get their feedback live

Getting their sign-off on these things on the call accomplishes two things: you get the approval you need quickly, and you build transparency. They see real work is happening. That builds trust.

### Phase 4: Fill in the Gaps

This is your workshop section. Use this time to:

- Ask any follow-up questions you have from the onboarding form
- Get clarity on anything that was unclear or incomplete
- Handle any account access you still need (LinkedIn, email accounts, etc.)
- Confirm any remaining assets you need from them

### Phase 5: Action Items

Before you wrap up, clearly outline what happens next — for both sides. Tell them exactly what tasks you're completing before launch, and show them on your Trello board. Be explicit about what you need from them (review the leads list, approve the scripts, send a logo, etc.).

💡 **Tip:** A good rule of thumb: if the client isn't clear on their next steps at the end of the call, you haven't finished the call.

### Phase 6: What Happens After Launch

Walk them through what to expect once campaigns go live:

- How long it typically takes to see results
- Where they'll be able to find results and updates
- What to do when they get interested replies
- How you'll communicate going forward (Slack for day-to-day, calls as needed)
- When your first check-in call will be (60-day review is a solid default)

### Phase 7: Wrap Up

*"Great — if anything comes up, you know where to find us in Slack. We'll keep you updated as we make progress, and we'll be in touch ahead of our [60-day] review call. Let's get to work."*

---

## Key Principles to Remember

- **Lead the call.** Your client hired you as the expert. Show up that way. Have an agenda, follow it, and direct the conversation.
- **Transparency builds trust.** Showing clients your Trello board, your task list, your completed work — all of it signals that real effort is going in.
- **Churn comes from misalignment.** Clients leave when reality doesn't match their expectations. Set those expectations early, clearly, and honestly.
- **Practice makes it effortless.** Draft your agenda before your first call. Do a mock call with a partner. Record yourself on Loom. It'll feel scripted at first and second nature by the tenth.

---

## Onboarding Call Checklist

Before the call

- Onboarding form reviewed — note any gaps or questions
- Leads list prepared and ready to show
- Scripts written and ready for review
- Trello board set up with tasks visible
- Account access attempted — flag anything that needs 2FA on the call

On the call

- Introductions done — team roles and responsibilities explained
- Client intro completed — goals and expectations heard and aligned
- Screen share done — workspace walkthrough complete
- Leads list reviewed and approved
- Scripts reviewed and approved (or feedback collected)
- Account access sorted
- Gaps in onboarding form filled
- Action items clearly outlined for both sides
- Post-launch process explained
- 60-day review call scheduled
- Slack confirmed as the ongoing comms channel

After the call

- Notes added to Trello or client file
- Any remaining tasks assigned
- Follow-up message sent in Slack confirming action items

onboarding/client-onboarding-form.title: Client Onboarding Form
onboarding/client-onboarding-form.content:
# Client Onboarding Form

The onboarding form is one of the most important things you do with a new client. It gives you all the information you need to do your job well. The more detailed and thorough the information you collect, the easier and faster your client will get a result — and the better result it will be.

A great onboarding experience is essential to client retention. Done right, it can double or triple your retention rate. Done poorly, it sets the wrong tone and creates friction before you've even started.

💡 **Tip:** **Build this in Google Forms.** It's free, easy to share, and responses come back organized and easy to reference.

**When you send the form to your client, make this clear to them:**

> "Taking the time to fill this out correctly — usually around 15–20 minutes — gives me everything I need to get to work immediately and get you a result. The more detail you give me, the better I can do my job. If you fill this out thoroughly, I won't need to come back to you in a few days asking for more information, which means we move faster and you see results sooner. Please treat each question seriously and give as much detail as you can."

---

Section 1: Basic Information

1. Email address
2. Your full name
3. Best phone number to reach you for business purposes
4. Your website link
5. How would you prefer us to book meetings for you?
   - Book prospects directly onto our Calendly or other calendar app *(if so, please email us the booking link you want used after filling out this form)*
   - Get a written agreement on a day/time with the prospect over email and forward that email to us to book ourselves

Section 2: About Your Business

**6.** Who do you help? What result can you get for them? Is there a specific timeframe you get results in? How do you get results?

*Please answer each of these in fine detail.*

Section 3: Your Target Market

**7.** Who EXACTLY is your target market? Who would you like us to send personalised, targeted outreach to? What are their job titles?

*Please give as much detail as possible — location, revenue, company size, industry, seniority, etc. The more detail you provide, the better quality of meetings we will be able to get for you.*

Section 4: Technology Targeting

**8.** Do your prospects use any specific technologies that you know of that we can use to base our targeting on?

*Examples: Shopify, Klaviyo, Google Analytics, Salesforce, HubSpot, etc. If you're not sure, just say so.*

Section 5: Lookalike Audiences

**9.** Can you provide a handful of examples of past or current clients you would consider ideal? We will use their companies to build lookalike audiences.

*You can just provide website URLs. Even 5–10 examples helps enormously.*

Section 6: Who to Avoid

**10.** Are there any types of prospects you want to make sure we AVOID targeting?

*Please provide as much detail as possible so these don't slip into our lead lists — company types, industries, roles, company sizes, etc.*

Section 7: Keywords

**11.** Please provide as many keywords as possible that we can use when building your lead lists.

*Think about how your ideal prospects would describe their business, their job, or their problems. For example, if you're targeting SEO agencies, keywords like "SEO", "search engine optimisation", "organic traffic" etc. would work well. The more the better.*

Section 8: What You Sell

**12.** What EXACTLY do you sell to your target market?

*Explain in fine detail — in your own words. Tell us everything. What is the product or service, how does it work, what does the client experience, what's included, how is it delivered? As many details as possible.*

Section 9: Questions That Get Responses

**13.** Our email copy is often question-based. What questions can you think of that your prospects would likely respond to?

*Think about a scenario where you're on the phone with them. What questions would you naturally ask to uncover their situation or pain?*

Section 10: Pain Points

**14.** What are the main PROBLEMS your ideal clients have that you can solve for them?

*Please share at least 3–5 specific pain points your clients are known to have. The more specific, the better our copy will be.*

Section 11: Results & Outcomes

**15.** What RESULTS have you gotten for clients? How have you helped them solve the problems listed above?

*A big part of our outreach is writing to the end business outcome. Do you help clients scale revenue without adding headcount? Reduce costs? Improve performance metrics? Please be as specific as possible with real numbers where you have them.*

Section 12: Case Studies

**16.** What are your best case studies? Please provide as many as you'd like in this format:

> "We helped [company/company type] that [does xyz] get [xyz result] in [timeframe] by [doing xyz]."

*The more specific the result — percentages, dollar amounts, timeframes — the more compelling the outreach.*

Section 13: Guarantee

**17.** Do you offer any sort of guarantee to your clients? If so, what is it exactly?

*If not, just say no.*

Section 14: Lead Magnet & Free Value

**18.** Is there any type of lead magnet you have — or that we could create — to send to interested leads?

*Also, is there any free value you currently give or could give to prospects on discovery calls?*

Section 15: What Makes You Different

**19.** What makes you unique and different from your competitors? Why would someone choose you over a similar company?

*Be honest and specific. Generic answers like "great customer service" don't help us write compelling copy.*

Section 16: Meeting Goals

**20.** Approximately how many sales meetings, demos, or discovery calls are you currently generating each month?

**21.** How many meetings are you looking to generate from our service every month?

**22.** If we generate more meetings than your target, should we pause for the rest of that month — or do you have the capacity to take more? If yes, what is the maximum number of additional meetings you could handle per month?

Section 17: Success Definition

**23.** If you could get one thing out of your working relationship with us that would make it a smashing success, what would that be exactly?

hiring-team/hiring-general.title: General Info
hiring-team/hiring-general.content:
# General Info

Hiring your first employee is a big step. Do it right, and you set your agency up for growth. Here's what you need to know.

---

## Core Principles

### 1. Be Clear on What You Need

- **Define the Role:** Write a job description that clearly lists tasks, responsibilities, and required skills. Don't assume candidates will "figure it out." Clarity saves time.
- **Test for Fit:** Before committing, assign a small, paid task to see how they work. This ensures they can do the job and fit your style.

### 2. Treat People Well

- **Be Honest:** Set clear expectations about the job, salary, and hours. Don't overpromise.
- **Respect Their Time:** Pay on time, provide feedback, and acknowledge good work. If they're treated well, they'll deliver better results.
- **Cultural Sensitivity:** If you're hiring globally, especially from countries like the Philippines or Lebanon, take the time to understand cultural differences.

### 3. Move Quickly

- **Hire Fast:** When you find someone who fits your criteria, don't hesitate. Waiting too long risks losing good candidates.
- **Fire Faster (When Necessary):** If someone isn't meeting expectations despite your efforts to guide and support them, make the call. Keeping the wrong person drains your time and resources.

### 4. Build a Simple System

- **Use Tools:** Platforms like Upwork, OnlineJobs.ph, or Fiverr make finding candidates easier.
- **Document Processes:** Once someone is hired, provide written guides or videos to help them get started.

### 5. Be a Leader

- **Set the Tone:** Employees look to you for direction. Be clear, consistent, and available to answer questions.
- **Foster Trust:** Share your vision and involve them in your agency's success. Motivated employees are invested employees.

---

## Where To Hire From

We recommend [**OnlineJobs.ph**](https://www.onlinejobs.ph) to find any VA roles — inbox managers, list builders, admin, etc. You can also use [**Upwork.com**](https://www.upwork.com) or **Fiverr**, or source via groups like Instantly.ai's Facebook group.

| Platform | Pros | Cons |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **OnlineJobs.ph** | Huge database, great filters, lots of agency-relevant experience (inbox managing, list building, admin), looking for full-time work | Philippines only; costs $69 |
| **Upwork** | Many countries available, great filters, free to post | Policy requires keeping payment within platform |
| **Fiverr** | Many countries available | Focused on gigs/short-term rather than full-time (though possible) |

---

## Compensation Structure

We recommend either a **base salary with commission per booked call** or a **fully base salary**.

The base + commission model incentivizes performance and reduces your risk as an employer — but can create a moral hazard where employees over-focus on booking volume at the expense of quality. A fully base salary provides stability and lets employees prioritize quality without pressure.

### How Much is a VA?

You will pay between **$4–8/hour** for a VA who is not extremely specialized. Most will be in the **$3–7/hour** range.

$6/hr × 8 hrs/day = **$48/day** → $48 × 21 avg business days/month = **~$1,008/month**

---

## Time Tracking

We highly recommend **HubStaff** — it tracks their time and lets you know exactly how much they're owed.

- Track time
- Set shift schedules + breaks
- Screenshots every 10 min *(check occasionally to ensure they aren't working for someone else during their shift)*
- Mouse activity tracking *(turn this off)*
- Timesheets showing exact amount to pay

You can use **Clockify** for a cheaper and simpler alternative.

---

## How to Pay Them

We recommend paying employees through **Wise**: lowest fees, quickest transfer speeds, numerous deposit options, and always reliable.

⚠️ **Warning:** Don't ever mess around with payroll. **Pay on time, every time.**

hiring-team/key-roles.title: Key Roles & Job Posting Tips
hiring-team/key-roles.content:
# Key Roles & Job Posting Tips

When writing a job posting, keep these principles in mind:

- **Explain what they would be doing** in a way they would understand — their responsibilities and most common tasks. For example, if you're hiring an inbox manager they might not know what that is — but if you say it's B2B email chat support, appointment setting, or SDR email lead reply handling, they may have years of experience in that.
- **Explain who your company is.** Give them an idea of what it will be like to work with you. Most are used to working with big agencies or call centres — they'll be surprised and interested in working for a company of 2–5.
- **State the time zone** you are in, and make sure they focus on your time, not theirs. This makes it much easier for you, and if they fail to listen to your instructions they are discarded.
- **Give specific and detailed instructions to apply.** The reason is you are simply seeing if they follow your instructions or not. If they do not follow instructions during the application, why would you hire them? If you tell them to change the subject line of their application to "silly dinosaur", but the subject line says something else, you know they are not a good person to hire.

hiring-team/hiring-inbox-manager.title: Hiring an Inbox Manager
hiring-team/hiring-inbox-manager.content:
# Hiring an Inbox Manager

Make a copy of this template and customise it for your own agency before posting.

💡 **Tip:** [**Google Docs — Hiring Inbox Manager Template**](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1gM3eHCLfk31uobk7MnW4QeyKac3CA_RB5-9qHBWZc5Y/edit)

---

📋 Full Job Posting Template

I am looking for an Inbox Manager, which is very similar to email support. You will be responding to incoming emails from leads we have contacted for B2B services. You will be qualifying/disqualifying the leads, communicating with them, and then scheduling sales appointments with the qualified leads.

**Your Main Responsibilities:**

- Oversee multiple client inboxes (Gmail inboxes)
- Respond & communicate with leads
- Follow up with leads
- Keep track of leads with a simple CRM
- Schedule meetings for our clients (through Calendly)
- Keep up-to-date reports on the incoming emails

**The Ideal Candidate:**

- Superb English written skills
- Willingness to learn from both SOPs and 1-on-1s
- Comfortable with frequent communication with the team
- Must be trainable, and takes direction well
- Some experience in virtual customer service, chat/email support, etc.

**Our Company:** Works with other businesses to get them sales calls/meetings. We do this by sending emails on our client's behalf, and then handling the responses from those emails. Every inbox will receive replies from leads we have contacted, and your main role will be to communicate with the leads that have replied.

**About Me:** My name is **[X]** and I run my business by myself. We work with companies in **[X]** countries. I am growing a Lead Generation Agency that specializes in cold email. We will communicate through Slack and Zoom. You must have access to a microphone and camera for the duration of your shift.

**Hours:** Part-Time, Monday–Friday. 10–20 hours per week / 2–4 hours per day. We run on Mountain Time (MT), which is 14 hours behind Manila Time. You must be available for at least 2 hours between 7am and 11am Mountain Time.

**Pay:** TBD — minimum $4.00 USD/hour. If you have experience in email support and/or customer service, this rate could be higher. Pay calculated by HubStaff's time-tracking system.

---

**To Apply For This Job:** *(Follow these instructions exactly. They are set up to weed out candidates that do not follow instructions.)*

1. Change the subject line to **"strawberry milkshake"**
2. At the top of your message write 4–5 sentences on why you would like this position, and why you are a good fit. Do not write more than that.
3. Under your 4–5 sentences, put the answers to the following questions and include the numbers to make it easy for me to read.

**Required questions:**

1. What days and times (in Mountain Time) are you available?
2. What is your experience with chat/email support?
3. Are you comfortable with using time-tracking software (we use HubStaff)?

**Bonus questions** *(Answer "No" if you don't have experience):*

1. Have you worked with very small companies (1–4 people) before?
2. Do you have any experience with Cold Email or Lead Generation? If so, explain.
3. Do you have experience with: Scraping (building lead lists), Spreadsheet organization, Setting up email campaigns?
4. Do you have experience with: Apollo.io, LinkedIn Sales Navigator, Google Sheets, Instantly.io, Slack, Zapier?

hiring-team/application-process.title: Application Process
hiring-team/application-process.content:
# Application Process

We recommend a 4-step hiring process: **Job Posting → Application Form → Loom Video → Interview**

This funnel is a filtering process to save you lots of time. You want to end up interviewing 3–5 people, after getting 50–100 job applications. Each step weeds out those who are lazy, incompetent, or unqualified.

---

## Step 1: Job Posting

- Give them all the info they would want to know, and all the info you want them to know about the job.
- Give them specific instructions to apply, so you can see if they follow them or not.
- You will get a lot of applications, so do not spend too much time reading each one. Just scan to see if they followed your instructions and have decent experience.

---

## Step 2: Application Form

Use **Google Forms** or **Typeform** to create a free application form.

**Watch out for these red flags:**

- Rushed/one-sentence answers
- Lack of experience or skills
- Poor English
- Incompetence

📋 Sample Application Form Questions (Inbox Manager Role)

**Basic Info:**

- Email, First and Last Name, City or Town
- Post a link to your Resume/CV
- Do you have access to a web camera and microphone for the duration of your shift?
- What is your internet speed? Post a screenshot of proof.
- If you lose internet at your house, do you have other means of using the internet? How?
- Are you currently working? If so, explain your current job and its hours.

**Work Experience & Skills:**

- Describe [briefly] your experience working as a VA. Give details about how many people/companies you've worked with, how long, and what your tasks were.
- Describe [in detail] your experience with email/chat support.
- Describe [in detail] your experience as an SDR, in sales or business development, or with lead generation.
- Do you have experience communicating with leads or prospects in a B2B setting? If so, explain [in detail].

**Other Skills:**

- Experience sending cold emails and/or setting up email campaigns?
- Experience in list scraping (building lead lists)?
- Experience organizing spreadsheets or using Google Sheets?
- Experience in email tech/backend work — e.g. setting up DNS records, forwarding domains?

**Software Knowledge** *(explain your knowledge and experience, or say "None"):*

- Apollo.io — lead database software
- Instantly.ai — cold email platform
- Zapier — automation software
- Notion — like Google Drive with more functionality
- Any other similar software

**Hours & Wages:**

- How many hours are you looking for per week? (This position is hiring for 20–40 hours/week, preferably 40)
- What time of day would you be available? (Use Mountain Time)
- What days of the week would you be available? (Monday–Friday required. Weekends a bonus)
- What hourly rate do you request? (Starting wage is $4.00/hour, can increase with experience)

**Final Questions:**

- Why do you think you'd be a great fit for this position?
- What is your one biggest strength that would help you succeed?
- Give one weakness — something you want to improve upon.
- Are you willing to do other types of work if needed? (full training provided)
- Are your written and oral English skills good enough to communicate with prospects through email?
- Is there anything I should know about you specifically with regards to this job?
- **Who would win in a fight between a tiger and a gorilla? Explain why.** *(There is no wrong answer.)*

---

## Step 3: Loom Video

Ask shortlisted candidates to record a **1-minute (or longer) video** going over 3–5 of their main qualities.

**Why this works:**

- You get to see and hear from them without scheduling a 30-minute interview
- You can immediately disqualify people after listening to them speak for just 2 minutes
- They must explain their answers out loud — a lot more truth about their skills and experience comes out when they speak vs. write

💡 **Tip:** Tell them Loom has a free version they can use to record and share their video. *Don't tell them this: if they can't figure out how to use Loom, they're probably not a good fit.*

---

## Step 4: Interview

Set up a **Calendly event** for the interview. Make it open for a **2-day window only**, with plenty of time slots throughout the day so candidates can genuinely find a time that works.

Most candidates will be nervous — especially those hiring from the Philippines. Try to put them at ease right away. This allows them to open up about their skills and experience.

📋 Interview Question Framework

**Opening:**

- Why are you interested in this position?
- How many hours a week do you want to work?

**Other Employers:**

- Do you have another job right now?
- If so, will you be leaving that job or working both?
- Will it conflict with this role?
- Describe your relationship with your current employer — how do you communicate, how do they give you work?

**Role-Specific Experience** *(swap in your relevant questions):*

- How many years of experience do you have in [relevant area]?
- How many clients/accounts have you worked on?
- What did you personally do vs. what did others handle?
- What kind of clients/companies have you worked for? What were the results like?
- What research do you do before starting a new project?
- How do you structure your work and manage multiple accounts?
- How many accounts/clients can you handle in 20–40 hours per week while maintaining full attention to each?

**Working With Me:**

- What do you think I'll need from you in this role?
- Are you able to explain and teach your processes to me?
- How do you feel about working in a small company with a less experienced leader?

**Personal:**

- Tell me about where you live and your family.
- What do you like to do outside of work?
- Tell me about a hard decision you had to make recently.

**Wrap Up:**

- What salary/hourly rate are you hoping to get for this position?
- Why do you think that rate is fair?
- Do you have any questions for me?

> "I have interviews until the end of Friday. I'll make my decision that night or the next day, and reach out to the winning candidate with an offer. I'll also let the other candidates know the position has been filled."

hiring-team/employee-onboarding.title: Employee Onboarding & Training
hiring-team/employee-onboarding.content:
# Employee Onboarding & Training

## Expectations & KPIs

Set these on their first day during onboarding:

- Show up on time
- Respect the company and its interests
- Follow directions carefully
- Always ask for help, even if they've been told 8 times
- Respond to leads within 5 min (or whatever job-specific KPIs are required)
- Take accountability of the work

---

## SOPs

SOPs are the core of onboarding new employees. Employees can refer to these repeatedly, which is great for them and vastly reduces the time spent by *you*. Use our resources for any SOPs you may need.

---

## Onboarding

Give them all the logins needed to do their work. Give them a quick and easy way to contact you — we recommend Slack or WhatsApp. Go over their main work with them, even if you have SOPs. It's important for them to understand how you want things done.

Make sure they have all of these things:

- The tools needed to do their job (software, logins, etc.)
- The resources needed for their job (SOPs, guides, etc.)
- Clear tasks, goals, or KPIs for them to work on
- A method to contact you directly, for direction or assistance
- A positive work environment (a good boss, good coworkers)
- A sense of accomplishment — people want to feel like they are doing good work and contributing
- A reliable source of income

⚠️ **Warning:** **Most important: never, ever be late in paying them. Ever.**

hiring-team/social-hiring-funnels.title: Social Media Hiring Funnels
hiring-team/social-hiring-funnels.content:
# Social Media Hiring Funnels

If you want A-players on your team, you have to treat hiring the same way you treat attracting top clients — you have to market to people.

Posting a job listing and waiting for the best candidates to find it doesn't work. The best candidates have options. If you want them to choose you, you have to make an offer compelling enough to stop them in their tracks.

---

## The Core Idea: Hiring is Marketing

> Hiring top talent requires the same thinking as closing premium clients. You're not just posting a job — you're selling an opportunity.

When you treat your job posting like a VSL and your application page like a landing page, the quality of applicants goes up dramatically. The right people get excited. The wrong people self-select out.

---

## The Hiring Funnel Structure

### Step 1: The Social Media Post

Start with a short, punchy post across all your social platforms. Keep it focused:

- **Who you're hiring** — be specific about the role
- **What they'll be doing** — and why it's meaningful
- **Where the role is** — remote, in-person, hybrid
- **A link** — to your full hiring page (see Step 2)
- **A short video** — this is the key differentiator (see below)

The post should sell the opportunity, not just describe it. **The video** is what separates this from a standard job post. Think of it as a mini-VSL — 1–3 minutes that walks potential candidates through: who you are and what your business does, the mission and where you're going, what this role looks like day-to-day, and why this is a team worth joining.

💡 **Tip:** Research consistently shows that pitch videos increase the caliber of applicants and likelihood of a great culture fit significantly. If you can only do one thing differently in your hiring process, make it this.

### Step 2: The Hiring Landing Page

Once someone clicks your link, they land on a page — this can be a Notion doc, a simple website, or anything cleanly designed — that functions like a sales page for the opportunity. Include:

- **The role details** — title, full-time/part-time, remote, compensation
- **The video again** — for anyone who missed it in the social post
- **What the role involves** — clear expectations and responsibilities
- **Requirements** — what experience or qualities you need (be specific — this filters out bad fits early)
- **About your team/company** — sell the mission, not just list who's on the team
- **Perks and benefits**
- **A clear CTA** — "Apply here" with a link to your application form

This page does your pre-selling for you. By the time someone hits "Apply," they should already be bought in.

### Step 3: The Application Form

Use Google Forms or Typeform. Keep it simple — you're going to get a lot of submissions and you need to be able to review them efficiently. Include:

- Name, email, location
- CV or portfolio link (optional — depends on the role)
- 2–3 specific written questions that reveal how they think
- **A role-specific video or task assessment** — this is the most important part

**The Assessment:** Ask them to do something directly related to the role — you're looking for effort, creativity, and whether they understand what the role actually involves.

- Hiring a copywriter? Ask them to write a short sample email.
- Hiring an inbox manager? Ask them how they'd handle a specific response scenario.
- Hiring a content person? Ask them what ideas they'd implement if they had full control over one of your channels.

**Require a short Loom video** (1–2 minutes) where they introduce themselves and explain why they want the role. Anyone who skips the video gets automatically disqualified.

---

## The Sourcing Strategy

Don't just post once and wait. Put it everywhere:

- All your social media platforms
- Your email list
- Your existing community or group (if you have one)
- Your current team — ask them to share it
- Industry peers and connections who might know great candidates
- Relevant Facebook groups, Slack communities, or forums

---

## The Filtering Process

1. **Quickly scan** for obvious disqualifiers — didn't submit the video, poor written communication, clearly not qualified
2. **Watch the Loom videos** for the remaining applicants — 1–2 minutes each, very fast to get through
3. **Review the assessment answers** for the top candidates — look for creativity and genuine effort
4. **Handpick 3–5 people** to move to interviews

---

## The Full Funnel at a Glance

| Stage | What It Is | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Social Post + Video | Short post with a mini-VSL | Attract and excite the right candidates |
| Landing Page | Notion doc or webpage | Pre-sell the opportunity, filter out poor fits |
| Application Form | Google Form with questions + Loom video | Assess quality and follow-through |
| Review | Watch videos, read answers | Narrow to top 3–5 candidates |
| Interview | Calendly event, structured conversation | Final selection |

---

## Why This Works

Building the right team is the single biggest lever you can pull to scale your business. More than lead generation, more than marketing, more than sales — the right people compound everything else you're doing.

When you treat hiring like a marketing funnel, you stop settling for whoever applied and start selecting from people who are genuinely excited to work with you. That changes everything about the quality of work, the retention, and the culture you build.

operations-scaling/ops-strategy.title: Strategy & Planning
operations-scaling/ops-strategy.content:
# Strategy & Planning

Scaling your business is more than just "growing it." It's about growing smart and staying agile. Here's how to plan and strategise for growth so that it doesn't only look good on paper but actually works in action.

---

## 1. Vision and Goals

First you've got to know where you're wanting to go. What's the big dream for your business? More clients? Bigger team? Being the go-to name in lead gen? Nail down your vision and set some solid goals that'll take you there.

💡 **Tip:** **Action:** Write down your business's vision. Make it punchy and inspiring. Then list 3–5 big goals for the next year. Keep them clear and trackable.

---

## 2. Know Your Playground

You can't win if you don't know the game. Who needs your service? What's happening in your industry? Research market trends and competitor movements to see where you should be focusing your efforts.

⚠️ **Warning:** **Action:** Spend a day researching your ideal clients and main competitors. Jot down any gaps in their game you can capitalise on.

---

## 3. Resources Check

Scaling means doing more — and for that, you need more resources: money, tools, and team. Figure out what you've got and what you need to hit your goals.

⚠️ **Warning:** **Action:** Do an inventory. Need more people? More tech? Outline what's missing and start planning to get it based on the goals you've set.

---

## 4. Make Your Model Scalable

Your business setup should be flexible and scalable — meaning no matter how much you grow, it won't break. Make sure your pricing and processes are set up for easy scaling.

⚠️ **Warning:** **Action:** Identify any bottlenecks or hard-to-scale aspects of your business and fix them.

---

## 6. Partner Up

Partnerships can be gold. They can get you in front of the right eyes and add value to what you offer without doubling your workload.

💡 **Tip:** **Action:** List potential partners who align with your approach and offer complementary services. Reach out and start a conversation.

---

## 7. Plan for Rainy Days

Have a plan for WHEN — not IF — things go sideways. Hard times are inevitable, but it's how we prepare for them that matters.

⚠️ **Warning:** **Action:** Think about the "what ifs." What if a big client bails? What if there's a tech hiccup? Sketch out a couple of backup plans.

operations-scaling/creating-sops.title: Creating SOPs
operations-scaling/creating-sops.content:
# Creating SOPs

SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) are the backbone of a scalable agency. They allow your team to do their jobs consistently without needing you for every decision, and they free you from being the bottleneck in your own business.

⚠️ **Warning:** **Coming Soon — SOP Examples & Tutorials**  
We are building out video tutorials and ready-made SOP templates to walk you through exactly how to create and structure your SOPs. Check back soon.

---

## SOP Ideas for Your Agency

Here are ideas for SOPs you should create as you build out your agency. You don't need all of these on day one — start with the ones most relevant to where you are right now and build from there.

Lead Building & List Management

- Using AI Ark, Discolike, or whichever lead building tools you're using
- Creating searches with filters
- How to build lists for each client
- Building and exporting lists
- Formatting your lead sheet
- Adding leads to your ESP (Instantly, Smartlead, etc.)
- Finding emails from a list of domains
- Email verification process

Campaign Management

- Creating campaigns
- Scheduling campaigns
- Reviewing campaign performance
- Outreach quality checklist (prior to sending)
- Subject line best practices
- Managing domain deliverability
- What to do when a domain is blacklisted
- What to do when bounce rates are high

Inbox & Response Management

- Handling responses
- Templates for specific response types
- Naming conventions
- How to schedule calls with prospects
- How to rearrange calls with prospects

Client Management

- Client communication standards
- When and how to arrange calls with clients
- Call booked show-up sequence
- Call structure and checklist
- Onboarding process (including timeline of deliverables)
- Reporting standards
- When and how to send reports to clients
- Outreach performance sheet

Sales

- Qualifying prospects
- How to schedule calls with prospects
- How to rearrange calls with prospects

Team & Operations

- Hiring process
- Firing process
- Recording time (for VAs)
- Team meetings
- File storage and organisation
- Creating SOPs (yes, an SOP for making SOPs)

Finance

- Sending invoices
- Following up on unpaid invoices
- Reporting expenses
- Tracking expenses
- Amending agreements and contracts

Software Tasks & Usage

- Software tasks and usage guide for each tool your team uses
- LinkedIn outreach SOPs (if applicable to your service)

---

## Getting Ready-Made SOPs

You don't always need to build SOPs from scratch. Use any how-to video or guide in these resources as your own ready-made SOP. If there's a video walking through a process, that video *is* the SOP — just link your team to it and tell them to follow along.

operations-scaling/vertical-horizontal-s.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
operations-scaling/vertical-horizontal-s.content:
# Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling

When scaling your agency, there are two primary approaches to growth: **vertical scaling** and **horizontal scaling**.

---

## Horizontal Scaling

Horizontal scaling mainly focuses on increasing the number of clients your agency serves. For example, getting 50 clients on $2,000 retainers.

### Pros

- **Diverse Revenue Streams:** Working with a larger number of clients spreads your risk. If one client leaves, the financial impact is minimized.
- **Easier Sales Pipeline:** Lower price points can make it easier to close deals, especially for clients new to agency services.

### Cons

- **Operational Complexity:** Managing more clients means handling more accounts, which requires more systems and larger teams.
- **Higher Operational Costs:** Everything above will cost more money and reduce your profit margins.
- **Spread Focus:** Your attention, time, and energy will be divided among many clients, which can drive up costs and reduce efficiency.
- **Higher Churn Rates:** With more clients, the likelihood of client turnover increases, requiring consistent effort to replace lost revenue.

---

## Vertical Scaling

Vertical scaling involves deepening the relationship and revenue with a smaller number of high-value clients. For instance, instead of 50 clients paying $2,000 per month, you could have 10 clients paying $10,000 per month.

### Pros

- **Higher Margins:** Fewer clients mean lower operational costs, allowing for more personalized and premium services.
- **Deeper Relationships:** A smaller client roster enables you to invest more time in building trust and delivering value.
- **Simplified Operations:** With fewer clients, your team can focus more deeply on results and strategy, often improving outcomes.

### Cons

- **Risk Concentration:** Losing one high-value client can significantly impact your revenue.
- **Longer Sales Cycles:** Larger retainers often require more time and effort to close deals.
- **Increased Pressure:** High-value clients may have higher expectations and demands, which could strain your team.

---

## Which Approach is Right for You?

The choice depends on several factors:

- **Market Positioning:** If your agency serves a niche where premium services are in demand, vertical scaling might make more sense. Conversely, if your services cater to a broader audience, horizontal scaling could be more effective.
- **Operational Capacity:** Assess whether your team and systems are equipped to handle a high volume of clients or if they are better suited for deep, strategic work.
- **Risk Tolerance:** Horizontal scaling spreads risk across more clients, while vertical scaling relies on fewer, more critical relationships.

---

## Recommendation

💡 **Tip:** For most agencies, **vertical scaling will be the better path to growth.** By focusing on fewer clients who pay more, you simplify your operations, reduce stress, and unlock higher revenue potential with less effort. Invest in strong positioning and scalable infrastructure early on so you can seamlessly scale vertically and enjoy the rewards of fewer, better-paying clients.

operations-scaling/case-study-interview.title: How to Conduct a Case Study Interview
operations-scaling/case-study-interview.content:
# How to Conduct a Case Study Interview

If you've made it to the point of having a client case study, congratulations. It means you've generated meaningful results for a client — and that's a huge win you should be proud of. Now it's time to document it properly and cash in on the amazing work you've done so you can do the same for even more people.

---

## Why Conduct a Case Study Interview?

Most people don't think of case study interviews as a retention tool — but two out of three objectives here work directly toward retention.

1. **Draw attention to the client and make them feel good.** Featuring your client in a case study interview, hyping them up in front of your audience, doubles down on their success and makes them feel valued. This retains clients longer than results alone.
2. **Help other clients learn from this client's success.** By interviewing your successful clients, you can share those interviews with less successful clients to build belief that your service can work for them too.
3. **Drive new sales.** Showing prospects that your services produce real, meaningful results for people just like them is one of the most powerful things you can do for your sales process.

---

## Before the Interview

### Just Ask

This is the part most people overthink. Keep it simple. Ideally, reach out right after a big win:

> "Huge congrats on [specific recent win]. I'd love to feature you on my YouTube channel for a Client Spotlight interview, like I've done for [X] other clients. Let me know if you'd be interested and we can schedule a day and time."

Nobody turns down the opportunity to be featured — even if your channel is small. The appeal isn't the audience size, it's the recognition and the chance to show off their success. **Timing matters.** Ask after a win, not when campaigns are struggling or a client is frustrated.

### Craft a Unique Angle

The biggest mistake people make with case studies is making them robotic. **Stories sell.** Think about what makes this client's journey unique:

- Are they running their business while still in college?
- Are they based in a country others might think makes success harder?
- Did they start from absolute zero?
- Did they sign a whale client?
- Did they achieve a massive exit?

Every unique angle speaks directly to a specific type of prospect in your pipeline who shares that same story or limiting belief. Prepare your angle and craft your questions around it before the interview.

---

## During the Interview

### Recording Tool

Use [**Riverside.fm**](https://riverside.fm) to record. It records in 4K so your videos look crisp on YouTube — Zoom or Google Meet will look noticeably worse.

### Make It Podcast-Style, Not Robotic

You want the interview to feel like a conversation, not a questionnaire. Ask open-ended questions, go off script when something interesting comes up, and show genuine interest in their story. The more open-ended your questions, the better the story that comes out.

### The Core Question Framework

1. **Who are you and what do you do?** — Set the scene for your audience.
2. **How did you hear about my company/services?** — Always gets interesting answers and humanises the story.
3. **What were you struggling with before signing up? What was your business like?** — This is a goldmine. You'll hear pain points your current prospects share. *Try to get them to share specific revenue numbers here — let it come out naturally.*
4. **Why did you decide it was time to sign up?** — Helps fence-sitting prospects make a buying decision. If they waited, ask why.
5. **How did my services help you solve what you were struggling with?** — The bridge between their before and after.
6. **What have your business results been since joining? What wins or milestones have you achieved?** — *Push for specific numbers — revenue, leads generated, calls booked.*
7. **What's one piece of advice you have for other clients to get the most out of working with us?** — Tells you and your prospects what an ideal client looks like.
8. **What would you say to someone considering working with us?** — Leave this open-ended. Let them come to it in their own words.

### Length

Aim for **20–30 minutes** of recorded content. Anything under 10 minutes means you didn't get the story. The story is what sells — not just the before and after numbers.

---

## After the Interview

- **Upload to YouTube.** This is the main selling point for why clients agree to do these. Upload straight after running it through Riverside — no fancy editing needed.
- **Share with the client.** Send them the YouTube link and ask them to share it. They're incentivised to — it's their chance to show off their success publicly.
- **Upsell opportunity.** If the interview went well and the client is happy and sharing it — that's a prime moment to upsell. Don't miss it.
- **Distribute everywhere.** Social media, your website and landing pages, your VSL, email broadcasts to clients and prospects, your community, social clips and repurposed content. Don't record it once and forget about it — every case study you create should live everywhere in perpetuity.

---

## The Law of Reciprocity

If a service provider who has delivered real results for you asks you to do a case study interview — say yes. People who never give testimonials or case studies for others are often the same people who can't get their own clients to do them. Say yes more often. It comes back around.

---

✅ Quick Reference: Case Study Interview Checklist

**Before:**

- Identify the right client and timing (after a big win)
- Reach out with a simple, personalised ask
- Define the unique angle and story you want to tell
- Prepare your questions around that angle

**During:**

- Use Riverside.fm to record
- Follow the question framework but go off script naturally
- Keep it conversational and podcast-style
- Push for specific numbers where possible
- Aim for 20–30 minutes

**After:**

- Upload to YouTube (no editing needed)
- Share the link with your client and ask them to share it
- Look for an upsell opportunity
- Post across all social media
- Add to website, landing page, and VSL
- Send as email broadcast to clients and prospects
- Add to your case study library for ongoing use

offers-guarantees-case-studies/no-case-s.title: What to Do if You Don't Have Case Studies
offers-guarantees-case-studies/no-case-s.content:
# What to Do if You Don't Have Case Studies

Sometimes clients won't go on camera, give a testimonial, or the relationship ends too soon to capture it. **Case studies are crucial, but they can be tough to secure.**

Enter the **"Joe Rogan Effect."**

Film yourself in a podcast-style setup: camera at a 45-degree angle, mic in view, headphones on. Talk through the transformation you delivered with authority and professionalism — **no client needed!**

By highlighting the results you achieved, you can showcase your expertise and prove your value without ever relying on a client testimonial.

💡 **Tip:** **Genius.** This approach lets you create compelling social proof even when you can't get the client on camera.

---

## Step-by-Step

### Step 1: The Set-Up

Set the camera up so you're speaking at 45 degrees. If you want to put a mic or headphones on for a more authentic "Joe Rogan" podcast look, then do this.

### Step 2: The Questions

All you do — or get your client to do — is give answers in a conversational style to the questions below:

- **Introduction** — Who you are, what your role is, what your company does.
- **Setting the Scene (Before State)** — Who was your client and what were they trying to do?
- **Pain** — What were they struggling with?
- **Impact** — What did that mean for their wider organisation? Was it harming revenue goals, did it have a knock-on impact for other stakeholders?
- **Agitate** — What other things had they considered or tried? Why had these not worked out?
- **Solution** — What did you do? What was the process, step by step?
- **Outcome** — List any quantifiable benefits of the future state.

### Step 3: Tips

- **Descript** is a super easy-to-use video editor — it's free, and you can edit your video like a Word document. Insanely easy to use.
- **Invest in sound if you can.** EVERY iPhone can take great video. The biggest ROI in terms of video production is sound quality. A Rode Wireless Go Mic will cost you about £160 but will give you INSANELY good quality for your audio.

key-resources/deliverability-updates.title: Deliverability Updates: What's Working Now
key-resources/deliverability-updates.content:
# Deliverability Updates: What's Working Now

💡 **Tip:** This page is updated monthly with critical changes to cold email deliverability and infrastructure. **Last Updated: February 21, 2025.** Check this page at the start of each month to stay ahead of deliverability changes.

## 🚨 Critical Updates

💡 **Tip:** If this section is blank then you don't have to worry. However if there are updates, consider making changes immediately.

### Critical Update #1 — Copy Fingerprinting Has Accelerated (Again)

**What changed:**

- Google + Microsoft are flagging repeated phrasing **faster than October**
- Spintax permutations are being fingerprinted at the phrase level
- Entire sequences can be poisoned by **one flagged sentence** (often CTA, signature, job title, or phone number)

**Observed effects:**

- Campaigns dropping from ~1.5%+ to **0.4–0.7%** within 2–3 weeks
- "Burned Infra" often turns out to be **burned copy**

**Recommendations:**

- Test sending **without spintax** in your email body — it is being fingerprinted fast enough now that removing it entirely may outperform using it
- If you do use spintax, limit it to **signatures and permanent/static elements only**
- Rotate structures, not synonyms (words)
- Test copy every **week**

---

### Critical Update #2 — Outlook Filtering Behavior Has Changed

**What changed:**

- Outlook appears to apply **post-delivery LLM review**
- Emails can land in Inbox, then move to spam 30–60 seconds later
- Many placement tests are now **false positives**

⚠️ **Warning:** **Key insight:** Outlook placement tests ≠ real inboxing. Bot-based tests often open emails → send positive signals → skew results.

**Recommendations:**

- Use **real, aged human Outlook accounts** as placement tests (old Hotmail/Outlook accounts used for years)
- Ignore "green" placement tests unless verified by real inboxes
- Focus on **human replies only**

---

### Critical Update #3 — Outlook Reply Rates Dropping (Feb 2026)

**What changed:**

- Outlook has seen ~20% week-over-week drops in reply rates over the last two weeks
- Agencies that went all-in on Outlook after Q4 2024 (when it was outperforming Google) are feeling this hardest
- Expected to be temporary; monitor weekly

**Recommendations:**

- Don't abandon Outlook — this is a short-term fluctuation
- If you went heavy Outlook in Q4, rebalance toward 50/50 while this stabilises

---

### Critical Update #4 — Google Sender Bounce Rates Spiking

**What changed:**

- Google inboxes seeing 20–30% sender bounce rates across campaigns
- Accounts being logged out, phone verified, and disabled for spam at increasing rates

**Recommendations — Recovery process (tested — long-term rep impact still unknown):**

- Pause affected inboxes for 2 days
- Lower warmup volume
- Reintroduce at lower outbound volume

---

## 📣 Non-Critical Updates

### Update #1 — .info Domains Perform Identically to .com

**What we found:**

- Tested over 3 months, ~3 million emails sent
- Zero measurable difference in reply rates, inbox placement, or bounce rates vs .com
- .com is not a deliverability advantage

**Recommendation:** .info is a fully valid and cheaper alternative with no deliverability trade-off.

---

## 🔍 What We're Currently Reviewing

We're actively testing and researching:

- Premium warmup pools vs built-in warmup
- 30-day warmup vs 14-day warmup
- Aged domain setups vs new domains

Results will be published here once validated.
